Fix: Specify limits. “The Ministry shall have the power to issue rules and regulations to carry out the purposes of this Act: Provided, That any fees imposed under such rules shall not exceed [amount or formula], and any administrative penalties shall not exceed [amount or sanction], subject to the requirements of due process.”

11. Redundant provision. The bill restates something that is already in the BOL or an existing BAA, adding nothing.

Problem: “The Members of the Parliament shall enjoy parliamentary immunity as provided by law.” This adds nothing beyond what the BOL already provides.

Fix: Remove the provision. If the drafter wants to call attention to the existing immunity provision for context, do so in the explanatory note, not in the body of the bill.

12. Aspirational provision masquerading as law. The bill includes a provision that expresses a goal but creates no rights, duties, or enforceable obligations.

Problem: “The Bangsamoro Government shall endeavor to promote the holistic well-being of youth in the region.”

Fix: Either remove the provision or convert it into an enforceable obligation: “The Ministry of Social Services and Development shall establish a youth development program in every province and city within the Bangsamoro. The program shall include, at a minimum: (a)… (b)… (c)…”

13. Missing transition provisions. The bill creates a new regime but does not address what happens to existing arrangements.

Problem: A bill creates a new licensing system for a regulated industry but says nothing about businesses that already hold licenses under the old system.

Fix: Add a transitory provision: “All licenses issued under [prior law] that are valid on the date of effectivity of this Act shall remain in force until their expiration. Licensees shall apply for renewal under this Act at least sixty (60) days before the expiration of their existing licenses.”

14. Gender-exclusive language. The bill uses masculine pronouns as the default.

Problem: “The Chairperson shall submit his report within thirty (30) days.”

Fix: “The Chairperson shall submit a report within thirty (30) days.” Or, if a pronoun is needed: “The Chairperson shall submit his or her report within thirty (30) days.”

15. Using “and/or.” This construction is ambiguous. Does it mean both conditions must be met, or either one?

Problem: “A fine and/or imprisonment shall be imposed.”

Fix: Decide what you mean. If you mean both: “A fine and imprisonment shall be imposed.” If you mean either or both: “A fine, imprisonment, or both, shall be imposed.”

16. Passive voice obscuring responsibility. The bill says something must be done but uses the passive voice, leaving unclear who is responsible.

Problem: “The necessary funds shall be allocated.”

Fix: “The Ministry of Finance, Budget and Management shall allocate the necessary funds.”

17. No sunset clause for a pilot program. The bill establishes a temporary or experimental program but does not include an expiration date.

Problem: A “pilot program” is created with no stated end date, no evaluation mechanism, and no trigger for expansion or termination.

Fix: Add a sunset clause: “This program shall operate for a period of three (3) years from the effectivity of this Act. Within six (6) months before the expiration of this period, the implementing agency shall submit to the Parliament, through the appropriate committee, an evaluation report on the program’s effectiveness. The Parliament may extend the program upon review of the evaluation.”

18. Quorum and voting provisions that do not add up. The bill creates a body and specifies a quorum or voting threshold that is mathematically problematic.

Problem: A board has seven (7) members, and the bill requires a “two-thirds vote of all members” for certain decisions. Two-thirds of seven is 4.67 — not a whole number.

Fix: “A vote of at least five (5) members shall be required for…” Or specify: “a vote of two-thirds of all members, rounded up to the nearest whole number.”

19. General repealing clause with no specific repeals. The bill ends with “All laws, decrees, orders, rules and regulations, or parts thereof, inconsistent with the provisions of this Act are hereby repealed or modified accordingly” but does not identify any specific law being repealed.

Problem: Nobody — not the implementing agency, not the courts, not the public — knows what has been repealed.

Fix: Identify specific provisions: “Section 12 of BAA No. 5 and Section 8(c) of BAA No. 19 are hereby repealed.” If a general repealing clause is also desired, place it after the specific repeals as a safety net — but never as a substitute.

20. Incomplete effectivity clause. The bill omits or truncates the effectivity clause.

Problem: “This Act shall take effect upon its approval.” But under the BOL and the Constitution, laws require publication before taking effect.

Fix: “This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days following its complete publication in at least one (1) newspaper of general circulation within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao.”

11.4 Gender Sensitivity Review

Every bill filed in the Bangsamoro Parliament should be reviewed for gender sensitivity. This is not a matter of political correctness. It is a legal obligation rooted in the BOL, the Bangsamoro Development Plan, and the Philippines’ commitments under international law, including the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW).

Article IX, Section 11 of the BOL requires that “there shall be at least one (1) woman to be appointed to the Bangsamoro Cabinet” and that the Parliament shall enact a law “ensuring the representation of women in other decision-making and policy-determining bodies of the Bangsamoro Government.”12 Article XIII, Section 5 further provides that the Bangsamoro Government “shall guarantee full and direct participation of women in governance and development process, ensuring that women shall benefit equally in the implementation of development programs and projects.”13 The Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028 identifies gender mainstreaming as a cross-cutting strategy across all sectors. These commitments must be reflected in legislation.

What a Gender Sensitivity Review Covers

A gender sensitivity review examines the bill at three levels:

Language. Does the bill use gender-neutral or gender-inclusive language? The standard in modern legislative drafting is to avoid masculine pronouns as the default. Instead of “he” or “his,” use “the person,” “the official,” or “he or she” and “his or her.” Instead of “chairman,” use “chairperson.” Instead of “fireman,” use “firefighter.” The goal is not to police vocabulary but to ensure that the law, as written, treats women and men equally in its language.

Where the bill addresses a role that has historically been associated with one gender, consider whether the language inadvertently excludes the other. A bill on “fishermen’s cooperatives” may unintentionally signal that women who fish are not covered. Changing the term to “fisherfolk cooperatives” resolves the issue without changing the substance.

Substance. Does the bill affect women and men differently? If so, does it account for those differences? Consider:

  • Does the bill provide for maternity protections if it regulates employment?
  • Does the bill ensure women’s representation on boards, committees, and decision-making bodies it creates?
  • If the bill addresses land rights, does it recognize the property rights of women, including married women?
  • If the bill establishes benefit programs, are the eligibility criteria designed in a way that women can realistically meet?
  • Does the bill address gender-based violence if relevant to its subject matter?
  • Does the bill include sex-disaggregated data collection requirements for monitoring and evaluation?

Impact. Will the bill have a disproportionate impact on women, even if its language is facially neutral? A bill that requires applicants to appear in person at a provincial office during business hours may disproportionately burden women who have caregiving responsibilities. A bill that requires collateral for a loan program may disproportionately exclude women who do not hold property titles in their own names.

The Gender Review Checklist

Apply these specific checks to every bill:

  1. Does the bill use gender-neutral language throughout?
  2. Does the bill provide for women’s representation in any body it creates?
  3. If the bill regulates employment, does it include maternity and parental leave protections?
  4. Does the bill require sex-disaggregated data collection for monitoring purposes?
  5. Does the bill allocate a portion of its budget to gender and development (GAD) programs, consistent with existing GAD budget requirements?
  6. Does the bill contain provisions that could disproportionately disadvantage women, even if facially neutral?
  7. Have women’s organizations and gender experts been consulted during the drafting process?
  8. Does the bill align with the Bangsamoro Development Plan’s gender mainstreaming strategy?

11.5 Human Rights Review

The BOL contains a comprehensive set of rights provisions in Article IX (Basic Rights) that go beyond restating the rights in the Philippine Constitution.14 The 1987 Constitution’s own Bill of Rights (Article III of the Constitution) applies to all persons in the Philippines, including everyone within the Bangsamoro. The BOL’s Article IX adds Bangsamoro-specific rights — transitional justice, reparation for unjust dispossession, indigenous peoples’ rights, customary rights, religious freedom, human rights protections, labor rights, women’s protection, and the rights of settlers and non-Moro indigenous communities.

Every bill must be reviewed against these provisions. A bill that violates human rights — even unintentionally — is a bill that invites litigation, invalidation, and public opposition.

The Human Rights Review Process

Step 1: Identify the rights at stake. For every bill, ask: Which fundamental rights does this bill touch? A bill regulating assembly may implicate freedom of assembly. A bill establishing a regulatory body with investigative powers may implicate the right against unreasonable searches. A bill on social media may implicate freedom of expression. Identify all affected rights.

Step 2: Check the BOL Basic Rights and the Constitution’s Bill of Rights. Read Article IX (Basic Rights) of the BOL and Article III (Bill of Rights) of the 1987 Constitution, and determine whether any provision of the bill conflicts with any right recognized there. Pay particular attention to:

  • The right to due process and equal protection (1987 Constitution, Article III, Sections 1-2)
  • The right to freedom of expression, assembly, and association (Constitution, Article III)
  • The right against unreasonable searches and seizures (Constitution, Article III)
  • The rights of the accused in criminal proceedings (Constitution, Article III)
  • The right to privacy (Constitution, Article III)
  • The rights of indigenous peoples within the Bangsamoro (BOL, Article IX, Section 3)
  • The rights of women (BOL, Article IX, Sections 11-12)
  • Customary rights and non-discrimination (BOL, Article IX, Section 4)

Step 3: Check the bill’s penalty provisions. If the bill imposes criminal penalties, verify that:

  • The prohibited acts are described with sufficient specificity (no vague or overbroad prohibitions)
  • The penalties are proportionate to the offense
  • Due process protections are observed (notice, hearing, right to counsel)
  • The bill does not impose retroactive penalties
  • The bill does not create a penalty so severe that it amounts to cruel or unusual punishment

Step 4: Check administrative provisions for due process. If the bill creates administrative proceedings — license revocation, disciplinary proceedings, permit cancellation — verify that the affected person has the right to:

  • Receive written notice of the charges or grounds
  • Present evidence and witnesses
  • Be represented by counsel
  • Receive a written decision stating the reasons
  • Appeal the decision to a higher authority or to a court

Step 5: Check the bill against international obligations. The Philippines is a signatory to numerous international human rights instruments that are binding in the Bangsamoro. Check the bill against:

  • The International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
  • The International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR)
  • The Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW)
  • The Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC)
  • The United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)
  • The Comprehensive Agreement on the Bangsamoro (CAB), to the extent its provisions inform the interpretation of BOL rights

Common Human Rights Issues in Legislation

  • Licensing or permit requirements that restrict the exercise of fundamental rights (e.g., requiring a permit for peaceful assembly)
  • Broad investigative powers without judicial oversight
  • Administrative detention provisions that bypass the courts
  • Provisions that discriminate — directly or indirectly — based on ethnicity, religion, sex, or other protected categories
  • Penal provisions that lack mens rea (intent) requirements for serious offenses
  • Collective punishment provisions (penalizing a community for the acts of individuals)

11.6 BOL Compliance Review

The Bangsamoro Organic Law is the supreme law of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, subordinate only to the Philippine Constitution.15 Every bill enacted by the Parliament must conform to the BOL. A bill that conflicts with the BOL is ultra vires — beyond the Parliament’s power — and may be struck down by the courts.

The BOL compliance review is a systematic check of the bill against the relevant provisions of the BOL. It is more specific than the general legal authority check in the self-review checklist (Section 11.1.1). It requires you to read the BOL with the bill beside you and compare them, provision by provision.

How to Conduct a BOL Compliance Review

Step 1: Identify the relevant BOL provisions. Read the bill and list every BOL article that touches on the bill’s subject matter. For most bills, this includes:

  • Article V (Powers of Government) — Does the bill fall within the enumerated or general welfare powers?
  • Article XII (Fiscal Autonomy) — Does the bill involve taxation, revenue, or appropriation?
  • Article IX (Basic Rights) — Does the bill affect fundamental rights, including transitional justice, indigenous peoples, women, labor, or human rights?
  • Article VII (Parliament) — Does the bill comply with the rules on legislative procedure?
  • Article X (Bangsamoro Justice System) — Does the bill touch on Shari’ah jurisdiction or court procedures?

Step 2: Check for direct conflicts. Read each relevant BOL provision and ask: Does anything in this bill directly contradict what the BOL says? Examples:

  • Article XII, Section 9 of the BOL provides that the taxing power of the Bangsamoro Government “shall not extend to” income taxes (except on banks and financial institutions), customs duties, and several other categories.16 A bill imposing a regional income tax would directly conflict with this provision.
  • Article IX, Section 11 of the BOL requires the Parliament to enact a law “ensuring the representation of women in other decision-making and policy-determining bodies.” A bill that creates a governing body with no provision for women’s representation may conflict with this requirement.
  • The BOL requires that laws take effect after publication. A bill with an effectivity clause that bypasses publication conflicts with the BOL.

Step 3: Check for implicit conflicts. Some conflicts are not obvious on the surface. They arise when the bill’s provisions, taken as a whole, undermine the spirit or intent of the BOL even if no single provision directly contradicts a specific section. Examples:

  • A bill that concentrates power in a single agency to such a degree that it effectively undermines the decentralization principle embedded in the BOL
  • A bill that creates a regulatory framework so burdensome that it effectively nullifies a right recognized in the BOL
  • A bill that delegates so much authority to the executive that it effectively transfers legislative power away from the Parliament

Step 4: Check boundary issues. The BOL divides powers between the Bangsamoro Government and the national government. Some powers are exclusive to the Bangsamoro. Others are concurrent. Still others are reserved to Congress. The boundary is not always sharp. For bills that operate near this boundary, ask:

  • Is this subject among the fifty-five enumerated powers in Article V, Section 2?
  • If not, does the general welfare clause apply?
  • Is there a national law on the same subject? If so, does the BOL give the Parliament authority to legislate on the subject despite the national law?
  • Has the Supreme Court issued any ruling on the scope of Bangsamoro authority over this subject?

Step 5: Document your findings. Prepare a brief BOL compliance memo — even if it is just a one-page summary — stating which BOL provisions you reviewed, your conclusion, and any areas of concern. This memo serves as an internal record and can be shared with the sponsoring MP or the committee.

11.7 Bangsamoro Parliamentary Conventions

Every legislature develops practices and conventions that are not written in the formal rules but are understood by experienced Members and staff. The Bangsamoro Parliament, though young, has already established several such conventions. Knowing them will help you produce drafts that align with how the Parliament actually works, not just how the rules say it should work.

Conventions on Bill Drafting and Format

The explanatory note is expected but not required. Resolution No. 268 describes the explanatory statement as “optional” (Rule XVII, Section 7(a)).17 In practice, every bill filed in the Parliament includes one. The explanatory note has become a de facto requirement. Omitting it invites questions about the bill’s purpose and weakens the sponsoring MP’s position during deliberation.

The committee report accompanies every bill reported out. Section 16 of Rule XII requires committees to prepare a report containing discussions, views, amendments, and justifications.18 In practice, the committee report has evolved into a substantial document — sometimes longer than the bill itself — that serves as the primary reference during plenary deliberation. Drafters should anticipate contributing to the committee report by preparing amendment summaries and justifications.

Government bills take precedence. Resolution No. 268 provides that government bills have precedence over private member bills “without prejudice to consolidation.”19 In practice, the legislative calendar is driven by the government’s priority agenda. Drafters working on government bills should be prepared for accelerated timelines. Drafters working on private member bills should plan for the possibility that the bill may wait in the calendar for a longer period.

Consolidation is preferred over competition. When multiple bills cover the same subject, the committee typically consolidates them into a single bill or a substitute bill rather than choosing one and discarding the others. This means your draft should be written with the awareness that its provisions may be merged with provisions from another bill. Use clear, modular drafting that allows individual sections to be extracted and combined with sections from other bills.

Conventions on Legislative Process

Interpellation is substantive, not ceremonial. The period of interpellation in the Bangsamoro Parliament has developed into a rigorous examination of the bill. Members ask detailed questions about specific provisions, demand justifications for policy choices, and probe for weaknesses. Prepare for interpellation as if it were a cross-examination. The sponsoring MP must be able to explain every provision of the bill — which means the drafter must be able to explain it to the MP first.

Floor amendments during the period of amendments are serious proposals. In some legislatures, floor amendments are rarely adopted. In the Bangsamoro Parliament, floor amendments are a regular feature of plenary deliberation. Members who register with the Majority Floor Leader to propose amendments typically come prepared. Drafters should monitor the list of registered interpellators and amendment proponents to anticipate what changes may be proposed.

The Majority Floor Leader controls the flow of business. The Majority Floor Leader determines the order of business, maintains the list of interpellators, and sets the sequence in which Members are recognized to speak. Drafters should coordinate with the Majority Floor Leader’s office when preparing for plenary deliberation of a bill, especially regarding the timing of committee report presentation and the expected schedule for the period of amendments.

Swift Passage Procedure is genuinely exceptional. Resolution No. 268 provides for a Swift Passage Procedure that allows a bill to skip the detailed committee scrutiny and pass in seven working days.20 In practice, this procedure is reserved for genuine emergencies and bills that have broad consensus. Drafters should not plan around the assumption that a bill will receive swift passage unless there is clear and explicit guidance from the Chief Minister’s office or the Speaker.

Conventions on Relationships

The PRLS is a resource, not a gatekeeper. The Policy Research and Legal Services supports the Parliament and its committees with research and technical expertise. In practice, the relationship between individual MPs’ staff and the PRLS is collaborative. Drafters should engage the PRLS early in the research phase — not as a last-minute review — and treat its input as an important quality check.

Committee chairs have significant influence over the legislative agenda within their jurisdiction. A bill referred to a committee is effectively in the hands of the committee chair, who decides when it will be scheduled for hearing, how many hearings it will receive, and how aggressively the committee will push it toward a report. Drafters working on a bill should maintain a working relationship with the committee secretariat.

The Secretary-General’s office is the official custodian of the legislative record. All bills, resolutions, committee reports, and journals pass through the Secretary-General’s office. Drafters must ensure that the correct and final version of any document — bill, amendment, committee report — is the version filed with the Secretary-General. Versions circulated informally do not count. The official record is what the Secretary-General’s office has on file.

Conventions on Style and Substance

Numbers are written in words and figures. This convention — “thirty (30) days,” “Five Hundred Thousand Pesos (PhP500,000.00)” — is universal in Bangsamoro legislation. Do not omit either the words or the figures.

Monetary amounts use the Philippine peso with the PhP abbreviation. The format is: “Two Hundred Thousand Pesos (PhP200,000.00).”

The enacting clause is standard. The timeless form is “Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament:” — during the BTA period, the form is “Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority in Parliament assembled:” Do not vary from the applicable standard formulation.

The effectivity clause follows a standard form. “This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days following its complete publication in at least one (1) newspaper of general circulation within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao.” This formulation tracks the requirement under the BOL and is used consistently in enacted BAAs.

Section headings are italicized in enrolled bills. When the bill is enrolled — prepared for signature by the Speaker and Chief Minister — section headings appear in italics. Follow this convention in your drafts.

The bill format uses “SEC.” for section numbering. Sections are numbered with Arabic numerals preceded by “SEC.” — for example, “SEC. 1.,” “SEC. 2.,” and so on. Articles are numbered with Roman numerals.

Why Conventions Matter

Parliamentary conventions are not law. They can change. They can be overridden. But ignoring them creates unnecessary friction. A bill that arrives at the committee in a nonstandard format requires the committee secretariat to reformat it. A bill that omits the explanatory note raises questions that could have been answered before filing. A bill that ignores the established terminology for Bangsamoro institutions signals that the drafter is unfamiliar with the Parliament’s work.

Following conventions is not about conformity for its own sake. It is about efficiency. A bill that looks like other bills, uses language like other bills, and follows the same structure as other bills lets the reader focus on what matters: the substance of the legislation. Everything else should be invisible.

11.8 The Legal Review Process

The self-review checklist (Section 11.1) and the specialized reviews (Sections 11.4-11.6) tell you WHAT to check. This section describes HOW to conduct a professional legal review — the process that ensures nothing is missed and every finding is resolved before the bill is filed.

The Six Dimensions of Legal Review

Every bill with legal citations should be reviewed across six dimensions. These are not checklist items — they are review processes, each answering a different question about the bill’s legal soundness.

Dimension Question What to Check
Accuracy Are quoted provisions correct? Compare every quoted provision word-for-word against the source text. Verify all section numbers, article numbers, and law numbers. Check that provisions are attributed to the correct law.
Currency Is the cited law still in force? Check whether each cited BAA has been amended or repealed by a later BAA. Check whether national laws have been modified. Check whether executive issuances have been superseded. Parliament has enacted over 89 BAAs since 2019 — legal references become outdated quickly.
Compliance Does the bill respect the legal hierarchy? Apply the 7-tier hierarchy (Chapter 1, Section 1.2) and the conflict resolution rules. Check that no provision of the bill contradicts a higher-tier source. Pay special attention to areas where the BOL requires consistency with national law.
Authority Does Parliament have the power? Trace the mandate-function-activity chain. Check whether the bill relies on explicit powers (Article V, Section 2) or implied powers (Article V, Section 3). If implied, verify the chain of reasoning is documented.
Sufficiency Is the legal basis complete? Check for gaps: are there provisions the bill needs but doesn’t cite? Are there cited provisions that don’t actually support what the bill proposes? Does the bill need an enabling BAA that doesn’t yet exist?
Risk What legal vulnerabilities exist? Identify potential constitutional challenges, conflicts with national law, weak implied-power arguments, or provisions that could be interpreted in unintended ways.

Not every bill requires all six dimensions at full depth. A simple bill renaming an office needs Accuracy and Compliance. A bill creating a new regulatory agency needs all six.

The QA Loop

A single-pass review is not sufficient. Errors missed in the first pass are caught in subsequent iterations. Fixing one error can introduce another — correcting a section number may reveal that the corrected provision doesn’t actually support the claim being made.

The legal review operates as an iterative loop:

  1. Run the selected review dimension(s) on the bill
  2. Report all findings, classified by severity:
  3. Critical: wrong verbatim text, wrong law cited entirely, provision contradicts higher-tier source, entity lacks authority for the proposed action
  4. Significant: wrong section/article number, cited law has been repealed, legal basis is incomplete
  5. Minor: punctuation differences in quoted text, citation format inconsistencies
  6. If Critical or Significant findings exist: read the actual source text, fix each error, and re-run the same review
  7. If zero Critical and Significant findings: the review passes — document the result
  8. If the review has been run five times and errors persist: the bill has a structural legal problem that cannot be fixed by editorial correction alone — escalate to the sponsoring MP and the PRLS for legal consultation

The loop is not optional. Do not declare a bill “legally sound” unless the final iteration of the review actually produced zero Critical or Significant findings. The temptation to declare victory after fixing the obvious errors is strong. Resist it. The non-obvious errors are the ones that surface during interpellation.

Common Errors Caught Only on Re-Review

  • Fixing a provision quote introduces a new identifier error (the correct text is in a different section than originally cited)
  • Correcting one provision reference reveals that a second provision was also misattributed to the wrong actor
  • Replacing a fabricated or paraphrased quote with the actual verbatim text reveals that the law doesn’t say what the bill claims it says — requiring a substantive revision, not just a citation fix
  • Updating a repealed BAA reference to the current law reveals that the current law has different requirements than the repealed one

Integration with Existing Reviews

The six-dimension legal review does not replace the self-review checklist (Section 11.1) or the specialized reviews (Sections 11.4-11.6). It supplements them:

  • Self-review checklist → covers structure, language, style, and internal consistency in addition to legal authority
  • Gender sensitivity review → covers gender-specific analysis not included in the six legal dimensions
  • Human rights review → covers rights-specific analysis
  • BOL compliance review → is subsumed by the Compliance dimension but with a more detailed BOL-specific process

Use the self-review checklist as your first pass. Then run the six-dimension legal review on the legal authority and citation aspects. Then run the specialized reviews for gender, human rights, and Shari’ah as applicable. The QA loop applies to all of them — if any review finds Critical or Significant errors, fix and re-run.

  1. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 2, art. V. ↩

  9. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 2(l), art. V. ↩

  12. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 11, art. IX. ↩

Back to Table of Contents
Glossary

Glossary of Legislative Terms

This glossary defines terms used in the Bangsamoro Parliament and throughout this manual. Definitions are drawn from the Bangsamoro Organic Law (Republic Act No. 11054), Resolution No. 268 (the Rules, Procedures, and Practices of the Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament), enacted Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts, and general Philippine legislative practice. Terms are listed in alphabetical order.

Adjournment. The termination of a session for the day, with the hour and day of the next session being set. An adjournment sine die is the final termination of a regular session.1

Amendatory Bill. A bill that proposes to amend, revise, or repeal provisions of an existing Bangsamoro Autonomy Act. The long title of an amendatory bill must identify the law being amended.2

Amendment. Any alteration or change to a bill or resolution by addition, deletion, substitution, or any combination thereof. Amendments may be committee amendments or floor amendments.3

Appropriation. The legislative authorization to spend public funds for a specific purpose. An appropriation provision in a bill identifies the funding source and the amount allocated for implementation.

Authorship Speech. The speech delivered by the sponsor of a bill during Second Reading, explaining the purpose, background, and provisions of the bill. Also referred to as the explanatory statement.4

Bangsamoro Autonomy Act (BAA). The legislative output of the Bangsamoro Parliament. A BAA is a bill that has passed through all stages of legislation and has been signed into law by the Speaker and the Chief Minister.

Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP). The medium-term plan that sets the development priorities and targets of the Bangsamoro Government. The BDP 2023-2028 is the current plan. Bills should align with the BDP where relevant.

Bangsamoro Expenditure Program (BEP). The annual budget of the Bangsamoro Government, equivalent to the national General Appropriations Act. The BEP allocates funds to ministries, offices, and programs for a given fiscal year.

Bangsamoro Organic Law (BOL). Republic Act No. 11054, also known as the Organic Law for the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (OLBARMM). The BOL is the charter of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. It defines the region’s powers, structure, and fundamental rights. All legislation enacted by the Parliament must conform to the BOL.

Bill. A proposed measure, or amendment or repeal of an existing law, presented to the Parliament for its consideration.5

Bill Number. The number assigned to a bill in the order it is received by the Office of the Secretary-General.

Blue Ribbon Committee. The Committee on Good Government, Accountability of Public Officers and Investigation. It has jurisdiction over matters including investigation of malfeasance, misfeasance, and nonfeasance in office.6

Caption. The heading at the top of a bill identifying the Parliament and the session. The caption includes the official designation “Republic of the Philippines, Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao, Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament.”

Certified Bill. A bill certified by the Chief Minister as requiring immediate enactment to respond to a public calamity or emergency. A certified bill is exempt from the three-day distribution requirement before Third Reading voting.7

Chief Minister. The head of the Bangsamoro Government’s executive branch, who exercises executive authority during the transition period. The Chief Minister signs bills into law, may certify bills as urgent, and presents the Government of the Day’s policies and programs to the Parliament.8

Committee Amendment. An alteration or change to a bill offered by the committee that scrutinized the bill during the committee stage. Committee amendments are presented to the plenary before floor amendments.9

Committee of the Whole. The act of the Parliament constituting itself into a committee to consider a bill or resolution, rather than referring it to a standing committee.10

Committee Report. A document prepared by a committee that contains the discussions, views, amendments, and justifications concerning a bill or resolution under its consideration. Members who participated in the final committee vote must sign the report.11

Committee Stage. The stage of legislation during which the concerned committee examines, scrutinizes, and deliberates in detail and line by line the provisions of a bill. The committee may propose amendments or revisions and produces a committee report.12

Concurrent Resolution. A resolution issued concurrently by both the Bangsamoro Cabinet and the Parliament expressing their sentiments on certain matters.13

Consideration and Deliberation Stage. The stage of legislation during which the Members of the Parliament deliberate and debate the committee report, including proposed amendments, in plenary session. This stage includes the period of interpellation and the period of amendments.14

Compliance/Reporting Clause. A provision requiring an implementing agency to submit periodic reports — typically annual — to the Parliament, through the relevant committee, on the status, progress, or outcomes of a law’s implementation. Compliance/reporting clauses are increasingly common in newer BAAs (e.g., BAA 73, 78) as an oversight mechanism.15

Conflict/Inconsistency Clause. A provision in a bill that addresses the relationship between the new law and existing laws on the same subject. The most common formulation is included in the repealing clause: “All laws… inconsistent with the provisions of this Act are hereby repealed, amended, or modified accordingly.” Some bills include a standalone conflict clause that specifies which law prevails in case of inconsistency.

Cross-Reference. A reference within a bill to another section of the same bill or to a provision of another law. Cross-references must be accurate and should be verified whenever the bill is revised.

Declaration of Policy. A section near the beginning of a bill that states the legislative intent and policy objectives the bill seeks to achieve.

Definition of Terms. A section of a bill that assigns specific meanings to words and phrases used in a technical or specialized sense. Definitions control the meaning of terms throughout the bill.

Dual Calendar. The system mandated by BAA No. 20 (Bangsamoro Dual Calendar Act of 2021)60 requiring all official documents and communications of the Bangsamoro Government to use both the Hijri (Islamic lunar) calendar and the Gregorian calendar simultaneously. The Hijri date appears first, followed by a slash, then the Gregorian date — for example, “Safar 26, 1447/August 20, 2025.” This requirement applies to BAA passage lines, official correspondence, memoranda, and other formal documents.

Deputy Speaker. Under Resolution No. 268 (Rule III, Section 3), eight Deputy Speakers are elected by a majority vote of all Members. In practice, the BTA has elected additional Deputy Speakers beyond the prescribed eight. Deputy Speakers assist the Speaker and take turns presiding over sessions when the Speaker does not preside. They serve as ex officio members of all committees with a right to vote.16

Effectivity Clause. The final section of a bill that announces when the law shall take effect. Under the BOL, laws take effect after fifteen (15) days following complete publication in at least one newspaper of general circulation within the BARMM.17

Enacting Clause. The clause that states the authority under which a bill will be enacted. The standard formulation is: “Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament:” — during the BTA period, the form is: “Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament in session assembled:”18

Enumerated Powers. The fifty-five subject areas listed in Article V, Section 2 of the BOL over which the Bangsamoro Government has legislative authority. These include education, health, agriculture, environmental protection, local governance, and many others.

Executive Session. A session that is generally closed to the public, involving matters of regional security, public safety, public health, or subject matters that may be offensive to decency and public morals.19

Explanatory Note. A document attached to a bill that sets out the purposes, background, and reasons for introducing the bill. Although described as optional in the Rules, it is included with every bill filed in practice.20

First Reading. The first stage of legislation, during which the Secretary-General reports the bill to the Parliament by reading its number, title, and author, followed by the Speaker’s declaration of whether it is a Government bill or a Private Member bill.21

Floor Amendment. An alteration or amendment to a bill offered by an individual Member of Parliament during the period of amendments in plenary session. Also called an individual amendment.22

General Welfare Clause. The provision in Article V, Section 3 of the BOL that gives the Bangsamoro Government the power to enact legislation for the general welfare of the Bangsamoro people, in addition to the enumerated powers. This clause broadens the Parliament’s legislative authority beyond the fifty-five enumerated subjects.

Germaneness. The requirement that an amendment must relate to the subject matter of the bill being amended. An amendment that introduces a provision not germane to the purpose of the bill — a “rider” — is not permitted.23

Hijri Calendar. The Islamic lunar calendar, based on twelve lunar months of 354 or 355 days, beginning from the migration (Hijrah) of Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from Makkah to Madinah. The twelve months are: Muharram, Safar, Rabi Al-Awwal, Rabi Al-Thani, Jumada Al-Awwal, Jumada Al-Akhirah, Rajab, Sha’ban, Ramadhan, Shawwal, Dhul Al-Qa’dah, and Dhul Al-Hijjah. Under BAA No. 20, the Hijri calendar is used alongside the Gregorian calendar in all official Bangsamoro documents. See also Dual Calendar.

Government Bill. A legislative initiative submitted to the Parliament by the Government of the Day for approval or amendment. Government bills have precedence over Private Member bills.24

Government of the Day. The executive branch of the Bangsamoro Government, led by the Chief Minister and the Cabinet. Government bills reflect the legislative agenda of the Government of the Day.

Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR). The detailed rules issued by the implementing agency to carry out the provisions of a law. Many bills include a provision requiring the promulgation of an IRR within a specified period from the law’s effectivity.

Interpellation. The period during plenary deliberation when Members of Parliament may ask the sponsor of a bill questions about its provisions, purpose, and effects. The Majority Floor Leader maintains a list of interpellators.25

Joint Committee Resolution. A resolution of two or more committees expressing the will or action of the Members on certain matters, requiring the majority vote of all the committees concerned.26

Journal. A concise and accurate account of the proceedings of what has taken place in every plenary session. The Journal is certified by the Secretary-General and is open to the public.27

Mandatory Review Clause. A provision that requires the Parliament or a designated body to review the law after a specified period — typically three (3) to five (5) years — to evaluate its effectiveness and determine whether amendments, extensions, or repeal are warranted. Mandatory review clauses are used for pilot programs, newly created institutions, or laws with evolving subject matter. Distinguished from a sunset clause in that a mandatory review does not automatically terminate the law.

Long Title. The formal title of a bill that describes its purpose and content. The long title must accurately reflect the bill’s contents and comply with the one-subject, one-title rule.28

Mace. A symbol of the Parliament’s authority, displayed at the foot of the Speaker’s rostrum whenever the Parliament is in session. It serves as the warrant for the Sergeant-at-Arms to enforce order.29

Majority Floor Leader. The Member who directs deliberations on the floor, chairs the Committee on Rules, prepares the Order of Business, and determines the order of recognition for Members to speak. The Majority Floor Leader also acts as the Whip for the Majority.30

Member of Parliament (MP). The official title of Members of the Bangsamoro Parliament.31

Minority Floor Leader. The Member who speaks on behalf of the Minority, serves as vice chair of the Committee on Rules, and is an ex officio member of all committees with the right to vote.32

Motion. A proposal made by a Member of Parliament upon which the Parliament or any of its committees shall take proper action. Motions may be main motions, subsidiary motions, incidental motions, or privileged motions.33

One-Subject Rule. The requirement that a bill shall only have one subject matter. An amendment that introduces a provision not related to the bill’s subject is a prohibited rider.34

P.B. No. (Parliamentary Bill Number). The bill numbering prefix adopted in the later sessions of the 2nd BTA Parliament (BAA 54 onward), replacing the earlier “BILL NO.” format. The P.B. No. is assigned by the Office of the Secretary-General upon filing. Examples: “P.B. No. 190,” “P.B. No. 341.” When a bill results from consolidation of multiple filed bills, the format is: “P.B. Nos. [N] and [N].”35

Order of Business. The daily agenda of the Parliament, prepared by the Majority Floor Leader and distributed to Members. The standard Order of Business includes, among other items, bills on First Reading, bills on Second Reading, the period of interpellation, the period of amendments, and bills on Third Reading.36

Parliamentary Committee. A permanent committee that exercises continuing responsibility in the legislative activity of the Parliament. Parliamentary committees are organized under the Rules and include the Committee on Rules, the Committee on Accounts, the Committee on Ways and Means, and others.37

Period of Amendments. The stage within the Consideration and Deliberation Stage during which committee amendments and floor amendments are proposed, debated, and voted upon.38

Point of Order. A motion that seeks to call the attention of the Speaker whenever a Member believes that proper decorum or procedure is not being followed. No Member may speak for longer than five minutes on a point of order.39

Policy Research and Legal Services (PRLS). The Parliament’s office that provides research and technical support to Members and committees in the preparation and analysis of legislation.

Popular Initiative. The process whereby a bill is proposed directly by the people as part of participatory democracy in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. The Parliament shall regulate popular initiative through guidelines.40

Private Member Bill. A legislative initiative introduced by a Member of Parliament who is not a Minister. Private Member bills are subordinate to Government bills in order of precedence.41

Privilege Hour. A sixty-minute period held every Thursday of the regular sessions during which any Member may speak on any matter of general interest. Privilege speeches are not to be interrupted except by a point of order or a motion to adjourn.42

Proposed Resolution. A document expressing the sentiment or opinion of the Parliament. It does not create or modify a law. Proposed resolutions are the standard legislative vehicle for expressing the Parliament’s position on non-legislative matters.43

Quorum. The minimum number of Members required to conduct business. In plenary sessions, a quorum is fifty percent (50%) plus one (1) of all Members. In committee meetings, a quorum is thirty percent (30%) of the committee members.44

Repealing Clause. A provision in a bill that identifies specific laws, sections, or provisions that are repealed or modified by the new legislation. A specific repealing clause identifies the exact provisions being repealed. A general repealing clause provides that all inconsistent laws are repealed — but this should supplement, not replace, specific repeals.

Residual Clause. The principle that powers not expressly granted to the Bangsamoro Government under the BOL are reserved to the national government. This is the counterpart to the enumerated powers. The drafter must ensure that a bill does not intrude upon powers reserved to Congress.

Resolution No. 268. The resolution adopted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament that provides for the Rules, Procedures, and Practices of the Parliament. It is the primary procedural authority for all legislative proceedings in the Bangsamoro.

Rider. An amendment that introduces a provision not germane to the subject of the bill. Riders are prohibited under the one-subject rule.45

Second Reading. The stage during which a bill is read in full (or copies are distributed), the sponsor delivers the authorship speech, and the bill is referred to the appropriate committee.46

Secretary-General. The officer accountable to the Parliament and the Speaker who manages the administrative operations of the Parliament, records proceedings, certifies Journals, serves as custodian of records, and performs other duties as prescribed.47

Separability Clause. A provision stating that if any portion of the law is declared unlawful or unconstitutional, the other provisions not affected by such declaration shall continue to be in full force and effect.48

Sergeant-at-Arms. The officer responsible for the discipline and security of the Parliament, including maintaining order in the session hall, implementing instructions of the Speaker, and serving processes issued by the Parliament.49

Shari’ah. Islamic law. Under the BOL, Shari’ah governs personal, family, and property relations involving Muslims in the Bangsamoro. The Parliament has authority to legislate on matters involving Shari’ah within the scope of the BOL (Article X, Section 4), including the jurisdiction and procedures of Shari’ah courts (Article X, Sections 7-10).50

Suppletory Clause. A provision that identifies a national law or other legal source that applies in a supplementary capacity when the bill itself does not contain an applicable provision on a particular matter. For example, BAA 82 (Bangsamoro Labor and Employment Code) provides: “In cases where this Code contains no applicable provision concerning labor, employment, or occupation, the Labor Code of the Philippines and other relevant social legislation shall be applied suppletorily.” The suppletory clause typically includes a proviso that the suppletory law must not be contrary to existing Bangsamoro laws.51

Short Title. A brief, commonly used name assigned to a law within its preliminary provisions — for example, “This Act shall be known as the ‘Bangsamoro Education Code.’” The short title is used for convenience in citation.

Simple Resolution. A measure used by the Parliament to take action affecting its own procedures, address urgent matters requiring immediate action, or express an opinion, sympathy, or commendation. Simple resolutions are not subject to the ordinary stages of legislation.52

Speaker of the Parliament. The Presiding Officer of the Parliament, elected by a majority vote of all Members. The Speaker opens, conducts, and closes sessions; signs all acts, resolutions, and bills; and exercises administrative supervision over the legislative personnel.53

Sponsorship Speech. See Authorship Speech. The terms are used interchangeably, though “authorship speech” is the term used in Resolution No. 268.

Statutory Committee. A permanent committee provided under the BOL and the Rules. The Parliament has seventeen statutory committees covering subjects such as finance, education, environment, health, and justice, among others.54

Substitute Bill. A complete replacement of a bill’s text offered as an amendment. A substitute bill must cover the same subject as the original bill — it may not introduce a subject distinct from the original. The substitute must be submitted in writing.55

Sunset Clause. A provision that sets an expiration date for a law or a program, after which it ceases to have effect unless renewed by the Parliament. Sunset clauses are used for pilot programs, temporary measures, or laws that require periodic review.

Swift Passage Procedure. A procedure for the speedy disposition of a bill in seven (7) working days that skips the detailed committee scrutiny stage. The Swift Passage Procedure allows cross-party or free conscience voting. It is used in exceptional cases.56

Third Reading. The final stage of legislation, during which there is a final debate on whether to pass the bill. No amendment may be considered at Third Reading. Printed copies of the bill must have been distributed to Members at least three days before voting, unless the bill is certified as urgent.57

Transitory Provisions. Provisions in a bill that address the transition from an existing regime to the new regime established by the bill. Transitory provisions may cover existing licenses, pending applications, current officeholders, and other situations that require a bridge between the old law and the new.

Ultra Vires. A Latin term meaning “beyond the powers.” A bill that exceeds the Parliament’s legislative authority — because it conflicts with the Constitution, the BOL, or invades powers reserved to the national government — is ultra vires and may be struck down by the courts.

Unfinished Business. Matters being considered by the Parliament at the time of adjournment of the preceding session. Their consideration shall be resumed until disposed of.58

Wali. The honorary head of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region who performs ceremonial functions, including opening the inaugural session of the Parliament through the banging of the agong.59

Whereas Clause. A recital in the preamble of a resolution that states the justifications, reasons, or factual basis for the resolution. Whereas clauses are numbered and arranged in a logical, deductive sequence.

Back to Table of Contents
Appendices

Appendices A through D

Appendix A: Enumerated Powers of the Bangsamoro Government (BOL Article V, Section 2)

The following powers are enumerated under Article V, Section 2 of Republic Act No. 11054 (Bangsamoro Organic Law).1 These define the scope of what the Bangsamoro Parliament can legislate on.

The text below reproduces the enumeration verbatim from the BOL. When drafting a bill, your first task is to identify which of these powers provides legal authority for your proposed legislation. If the subject does not fall within any of these enumerated items — and does not fall within the General Welfare Clause (Section 3) — the Parliament likely lacks the power to legislate on it.

SECTION 2. Powers of the Bangsamoro Government. — Subject to Section 20, Article X of the Constitution and this Organic Law, the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority over the following matters without prejudice to the general supervision of the President of the Republic of the Philippines:

  1. (a) Administration of justice
  2. (b) Administrative organization
  3. (c) Agriculture, livestock, and food security
  4. (d) Ancestral domain and natural resources
  5. (e) Barter trade and countertrade
  6. (f) Budgeting
  7. (g) Business name registration
  8. (h) Cadastral land survey
  9. (i) Civil service
  10. (j) Classification of public lands
  11. (k) Cooperatives and social entrepreneurship
  12. (l) Creation, division, merger, abolition or alteration of boundaries of municipalities and barangays
  13. (m) Creation of government-owned or controlled corporations and pioneer firms
  14. (n) Creation of sources of revenues
  15. (o) Cultural exchange, and economic and technical cooperation
  16. (p) Culture and language
  17. (q) Customary laws
  18. (r) Development programs and laws for women, labor, the youth, the elderly, the differently-abled, and indigenous peoples
  19. (s) Disaster risk reduction and management
  20. (t) Ecological solid waste management and pollution control
  21. (u) Economic zones, industrial centers, and free ports
  22. (v) Education and skills training
  23. (w) Eminent domain
  24. (x) Environment, parks, forest management, wildlife, and nature reserves conservation
  25. (y) Fishery, marine, and aquatic resources
  26. (z) Grants and donations
  27. (aa) Hajj and Umrah
  28. (bb) Health
  29. (cc) Housing and human settlements
  30. (dd) Humanitarian services and institutions
  31. (ee) Human rights
  32. (ff) Indigenous peoples’ rights
  33. (gg) Inland waters
  34. (hh) Inland waterways for navigation
  35. (ii) Islamic banking and finance
  36. (jj) Labor, employment, and occupation
  37. (kk) Libraries and museums, and historical, cultural, and archaeological sites
  38. (ll) Loans, credits, and other forms of indebtedness
  39. (mm) Mechanisms for consultations for women and marginalized sectors
  40. (nn) People’s organizations
  41. (oo) Power sector investments
  42. (pp) Public utilities’ operations
  43. (qq) Public works and infrastructure
  44. (rr) Quarantine regulations
  45. (ss) Registration of births, marriages, and deaths, copies to be furnished the Philippine Statistics Authority
  46. (tt) Regulation of the manufacture and distribution of food, drinks, drugs, and tobacco
  47. (uu) Science and technology, research councils and scholarship programs
  48. (vv) Social services, social welfare, and charities
  49. (ww) Sports and recreation
  50. (xx) Technical cooperation for human resource development
  51. (yy) Tourism development
  52. (zz) Trade and industry
  53. (aaa) Urban and rural planning development
  54. (bbb) Urban land reform and land use
  55. (ccc) Water supply and services, flood control, and irrigation systems

The General Welfare Clause (Article V, Section 3)2

The General Welfare Clause extends the Bangsamoro Government’s authority beyond the 55 enumerated items above. It is the basis for legislation on subjects that are not explicitly listed but are necessary for effective governance and the promotion of public welfare. The full text reads:

SEC. 3. General Welfare. — The Bangsamoro Government shall exercise the powers expressly granted, those necessarily implied therefrom, as well as powers necessary, appropriate, or incidental for its efficient and effective governance and those which are essential to the promotion of general welfare. Within its territorial jurisdiction, the Bangsamoro Government shall ensure and support, among other things, the preservation and enrichment of culture, promote health and safety, enhance the right of the people to a balanced ecology, encourage and support the development of appropriate and self-reliant scientific and technological capabilities, improve public morals, enhance economic prosperity and social justice, promote full employment among its residents, maintain peace and order, and preserve the comfort and convenience of its inhabitants.

Practical note for drafters. If you are relying on the General Welfare Clause rather than a specific enumerated power, say so explicitly in the Explanatory Note and the Declaration of Policy. A bill that rests on implied authority should make its legal reasoning transparent. Identify the express power the proposed legislation is “necessarily implied” from, or explain why the subject is “necessary, appropriate, or incidental” to efficient governance. The stronger you make this connection, the more likely the bill will withstand legal challenge.

Appendix B: Sample Bangsamoro Autonomy Act (Annotated)

This appendix presents a complete, fictional bill — the “Bangsamoro Community Libraries Act” — with annotations explaining what each part does and why it is there. The bill is not real legislation. It exists solely to show how a well-structured BAA looks from title to effectivity clause.

Use this as a reference when drafting. The annotations are enclosed in brackets and set in bold so they stand out from the bill text.

Republic of the Philippines Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY PARLIAMENT Bangsamoro Government Center, Cotabato City

BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY (THIRD REGULAR SESSION)

BANGSAMORO AUTONOMY ACT NO. ___

[ANNOTATION: This is the Caption. It identifies the legislature, the seat of government, the session, and the bill number. The BAA number is left blank during drafting — it is assigned by the Secretary-General upon enactment. Always use the correct session designation.]

Introduced by MP ______

[ANNOTATION: The authorship line identifies who filed the bill. If there are co-authors, list them all. This line appears before the long title.]

AN ACT ESTABLISHING A COMMUNITY LIBRARY SYSTEM IN THE BANGSAMORO AUTONOMOUS REGION, PROVIDING FOR THE CREATION, MANAGEMENT, AND FUNDING OF MUNICIPAL AND BARANGAY LIBRARIES, AND FOR OTHER PURPOSES

[ANNOTATION: This is the Long Title. It describes the full scope of the bill in a single sentence. The long title must be broad enough to cover everything the bill contains but specific enough to give fair notice of its subject. Under the “one subject, one title” rule, the long title cannot be misleading — every substantive provision must fall within what the title announces. The phrase “and for other purposes” is a standard concluding phrase, but it does not excuse provisions that are genuinely outside the bill’s scope.]

Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority in Parliament assembled:

[ANNOTATION: This is the Enacting Clause. It is the formal statement of legislative authority. The standard form during the BTA period is “Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority in Parliament assembled:” — used in approximately 50 of 89 enacted BAAs.3 Do not alter it. When an elected Parliament replaces the BTA, the formulation will change to: “Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament:”]

SECTION 1. Short Title. —

This Act shall be known as the “Bangsamoro Community Libraries Act.”

[ANNOTATION: The Short Title section gives the law a convenient name for citation. It always appears as Section 1. The short title should be concise and descriptive. Once enacted, people will refer to the law by this name — make it clear and memorable. The short title is set in italics and enclosed in quotation marks.]

SEC. 2. Declaration of Policy. —

The State recognizes the fundamental right of every Bangsamoro citizen to access information and knowledge. The Bangsamoro Government shall promote literacy, lifelong learning, and cultural enrichment by establishing a system of community libraries accessible to all residents of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region regardless of age, gender, religion, ethnicity, or economic status.

Toward this end, the Bangsamoro Government shall establish, fund, and maintain community libraries at the municipal and barangay levels, prioritizing communities with the least access to books, digital resources, and formal educational institutions.

[ANNOTATION: The Declaration of Policy states the legislative intent and the public interest the bill serves. This section answers the question: why does this law exist? It should be specific enough to guide interpretation but not so detailed that it duplicates the substantive provisions. Avoid making promises in this section that the bill’s operative provisions do not deliver. If the declaration says the government “shall” do something, the bill must contain a mechanism to make it happen.]

SEC. 3. Definition of Terms. —

As used in this Act, the following terms shall mean:

(a) Bangsamoro Community Library — a public library established under this Act and operated by the municipal or barangay government within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, open to all residents without charge for basic services;

(b) Basic Library Services — lending of books and other print materials, provision of reading space, access to reference materials, and digital access where available;

(c) Digital Resource Center — a designated area within a Bangsamoro Community Library equipped with computers and internet connectivity for public use;

(d) Library Development Fund — the fund established under Section 8 of this Act for the creation, maintenance, and improvement of Bangsamoro Community Libraries;

(e) Ministry — the Ministry of Basic, Higher and Technical Education of the Bangsamoro Government;

(f) Municipal Library Board — the body created under Section 6 of this Act that governs the operation of a Bangsamoro Community Library at the municipal level.

[ANNOTATION: The Definition of Terms section assigns specific meanings to words the bill uses in a technical sense. Define only terms that need defining — do not define words that carry their ordinary meaning. Each definition should work as a direct substitute: wherever the term appears in the bill, the definition should fit in its place. Use “means” for exhaustive definitions and “includes” for open-ended ones. Cross-references to other sections (like “Section 8 of this Act”) tie the definitions to the operative provisions.]

SEC. 4. Establishment of Community Libraries. —

Within two (2) years from the effectivity of this Act, every municipality in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region shall establish at least one (1) Bangsamoro Community Library located in or near the municipal hall or a similarly accessible public facility.

The Ministry shall identify priority municipalities based on the following criteria:

(a) absence of an existing public library;

(b) distance from the nearest university or college library;

(c) poverty incidence as determined by the most recent data from the Philippine Statistics Authority or the Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority; and

(d) population of school-age children and out-of-school youth.

Municipalities already operating a public library that meets the minimum standards prescribed by the Ministry shall be deemed compliant with this section.

[ANNOTATION: This is the first Substantive Provision. It creates the core obligation — every municipality must have at least one community library. Note how it sets a timeline (“within two years”), assigns responsibility to a specific body (“the Ministry”), establishes criteria for prioritization using a clear list, and provides a savings clause for municipalities that already comply. Each element is enforceable.]

SEC. 5. Barangay Reading Centers. —

The Ministry, in coordination with the municipal government and the Municipal Library Board, may establish Barangay Reading Centers in barangays that are geographically isolated or have populations exceeding ten thousand (10,000) residents.

A Barangay Reading Center shall be a satellite facility of the municipal Bangsamoro Community Library and shall provide, at minimum, a reading area, a rotating collection of books, and access to basic library services.

The Ministry shall issue guidelines for the establishment and operation of Barangay Reading Centers within six (6) months from the effectivity of this Act.

[ANNOTATION: This is a second Substantive Provision. It extends the library system to the barangay level but uses permissive language (“may establish”) because requiring every barangay to have a reading center would be impractical. The section defines what a Barangay Reading Center is, sets minimum standards, and delegates the details to Ministry guidelines with a clear deadline.]

SEC. 6. Municipal Library Boards. —

There is hereby created in each municipality a Municipal Library Board composed of:

(a) the municipal mayor, as chairperson;

(b) two (2) representatives of the local sangguniang bayan, to be designated by the vice-mayor;

(c) one (1) representative of the Ministry;

(d) one (1) representative of the local school division;

(e) one (1) representative from a people’s organization or non-governmental organization operating in the municipality, to be selected by the municipal mayor; and

(f) the head librarian of the Bangsamoro Community Library, as secretary.

The Municipal Library Board shall:

(1) oversee the operations and development of the Bangsamoro Community Library;

(2) approve the annual acquisition plan for books and materials;

(3) set the rules for library use and borrowing;

(4) submit an annual report to the Ministry on the library’s activities, collections, and usage; and

(5) recommend to the municipal government the annual budget allocation for the library.

Members of the Municipal Library Board shall serve without additional compensation.

[ANNOTATION: This is a third Substantive Provision that creates a governance structure. When a bill creates a body, it must specify: (1) composition, (2) manner of selection, (3) powers and functions, and (4) compensation or the lack thereof. This section does all four. The composition is listed with enough specificity that there is no ambiguity about who sits on the board.]

SEC. 7. Staffing and Training. —

Each Bangsamoro Community Library shall have at least one (1) full-time librarian who meets the qualifications prescribed by the Civil Service Commission and the Ministry.

The Ministry, in coordination with the Ministry of the Interior and Local Government, shall develop and implement a training program for community librarians within one (1) year from the effectivity of this Act. The training program shall cover:

(a) library management and cataloguing;

(b) digital literacy and basic information technology;

(c) community engagement and literacy promotion; and

(d) preservation of Bangsamoro cultural materials.

[ANNOTATION: This is an Administrative Provision. It addresses how the law will be implemented — who will staff the libraries and how they will be trained. Administrative provisions translate the bill’s mandate into operational reality. Without this section, the bill creates libraries but provides no mechanism for ensuring they have qualified personnel.]

SEC. 8. Library Development Fund. —

There is hereby established a Library Development Fund to be administered by the Ministry. The fund shall consist of:

(a) annual appropriations from the Bangsamoro Expenditure Program as provided under Section 9 of this Act;

(b) grants, donations, and contributions from domestic and international sources accepted by the Bangsamoro Government for library purposes; and

(c) income derived from fees charged for non-basic library services, such as photocopying, printing, and room rental.

The Ministry shall promulgate rules and regulations governing the management, disbursement, and accounting of the Library Development Fund within six (6) months from the effectivity of this Act.

All expenditures from the Library Development Fund shall be subject to existing auditing laws and regulations.

[ANNOTATION: The creation of a dedicated fund is a common mechanism in Bangsamoro legislation. This section identifies all funding sources, assigns a fund administrator, requires implementing rules with a deadline, and subjects the fund to audit. The last sentence is important — it ensures accountability and connects the bill to the broader legal framework for public finance.]

SEC. 9. Appropriation. —

The amount necessary for the initial implementation of this Act shall be charged against the current appropriations of the Ministry. Thereafter, such sums as may be necessary for its continued implementation shall be included in the annual Bangsamoro Expenditure Program.

[ANNOTATION: The Appropriation Clause identifies where the money will come from. There are two standard approaches: (1) charging initial costs to the implementing agency’s current budget and providing for future appropriations in the annual BEP, or (2) specifying a lump-sum amount. Approach (1) is more common because specifying exact amounts in the bill can create problems if costs change. Never create obligations without identifying a funding source — unfunded mandates are a common drafting failure.]

SEC. 10. Penalties. —

Any public officer or employee who misappropriates, misuses, or diverts funds allocated for Bangsamoro Community Libraries or the Library Development Fund shall be subject to administrative and criminal penalties under existing laws, including Republic Act No. 3019 (Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act), Republic Act No. 6713 (Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards for Public Officials and Employees), and applicable Bangsamoro civil service laws.

Any person who willfully destroys, defaces, or steals library materials, equipment, or facilities shall be liable for the cost of replacement or repair and shall be subject to penalties under the Revised Penal Code and applicable Bangsamoro laws.

[ANNOTATION: This is the Enforcement Provision. It specifies consequences for violations. Note that it references existing national laws rather than creating entirely new penalty schemes — this is good practice when national laws already cover the conduct. The section distinguishes between public officers (who face stricter accountability) and any person (a broader category). Be specific about which laws apply.]

SEC. 11. Separability Clause. —

If any provision of this Act is declared unconstitutional or invalid, the remaining provisions not affected thereby shall continue to be in full force and effect.

[ANNOTATION: The Separability Clause is standard in virtually every bill. It ensures that if a court strikes down one provision, the rest of the law survives. The language is formulaic — use this exact wording or its equivalent. Do not vary it without reason.]

SEC. 12. Repealing Clause. —

All laws, decrees, orders, rules and regulations, or parts thereof which are inconsistent with any provision of this Act are hereby repealed or modified accordingly.

[ANNOTATION: The Repealing Clause removes conflicting provisions from other laws. This is the “general repealing clause” formulation. If the bill specifically repeals an identifiable law or section of a law, name it explicitly before the general clause. A specific repeal is always stronger than a general one. Courts disfavor implied repeals — if you intend to repeal something, say so by name.]

SEC. 13. Effectivity. —

This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days after its complete publication in at least one (1) newspaper of general circulation within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region.

[ANNOTATION: The Effectivity Clause states when the law takes effect. Under the BOL,4 the standard rule is fifteen days after complete publication in a newspaper of general circulation within the BARMM. Some laws specify a different trigger — such as “upon approval” for urgent measures — but the fifteen-day publication rule is the default. Do not omit this section. A law without an effectivity clause creates confusion about when it becomes enforceable.]

Approved,

(Sgd.)

SPEAKER OF THE PARLIAMENT

*This Act, which is a consolidation of Bangsamoro Autonomy Bill No. ___ and Bangsamoro Autonomy Bill No. , was passed by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament on ___._

Chief Minister

[ANNOTATION: The Approval Block appears on enacted BAAs but is not drafted by the bill drafter. It is completed after the bill passes Third Reading, is enrolled, and is submitted for executive action. During drafting, you do not need to include this block. It is shown here only so you know what the final product looks like.]

EXPLANATORY NOTE

The Bangsamoro Autonomous Region faces a severe shortage of public libraries. According to the National Library of the Philippines, there are fewer than ten registered public libraries serving the entire region’s population of more than four million. Many municipalities have no library at all. Schools operate with limited book collections, and most residents have no access to reading materials outside the classroom.

This bill addresses that gap by mandating the creation of a Bangsamoro Community Library in every municipality, with satellite Barangay Reading Centers in geographically isolated or densely populated barangays. It creates a governance structure at the municipal level — the Municipal Library Board — to ensure local ownership and accountability. It establishes a dedicated Library Development Fund to sustain the system beyond annual appropriation cycles.

The legal authority for this measure rests on two enumerated powers under Article V, Section 2 of the Bangsamoro Organic Law: item (kk), “libraries and museums, and historical, cultural, and archaeological sites,” and item (v), “education and skills training.”5 The General Welfare Clause further supports the measure as one that promotes literacy, cultural enrichment, and access to information — all essential to the promotion of general welfare.

The bill draws on the experience of the Department of Education’s school library standards, the National Library of the Philippines’ guidelines for public libraries, and similar legislation in other ASEAN countries. It has been reviewed in consultation with the Ministry of Basic, Higher and Technical Education and endorsed by the Philippine Librarians Association — Bangsamoro Chapter.

The immediate passage of this bill is earnestly requested.

(Sgd.)

MP ___

[ANNOTATION: The Explanatory Note is the drafter’s argument for the bill. It is filed alongside the bill but is not part of the enacted law. A strong Explanatory Note answers three questions: (1) What problem does the bill solve? (2) Why is legislation the right response? (3) What does the bill do? It should cite the specific BOL authority, use data, and be concise — one to three pages is typical. The principal author signs it.]

Appendix C: Sample Resolution (Annotated)

This appendix presents a complete, fictional resolution with annotations explaining each part. The resolution is a simple proposed resolution — the most common type — urging action on a matter within the Bangsamoro Parliament’s concern.

Use this as a reference when drafting resolutions. Compare it with the bill structure in Appendix B to understand how resolutions differ.

Republic of the Philippines Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY PARLIAMENT Bangsamoro Government Center, Cotabato City

[ANNOTATION: The header is identical to the header used for bills. It identifies the government, the autonomous region, and the parliamentary body. This institutional identification is required on all official legislative documents.]

PROPOSED RESOLUTION NO. ___

[ANNOTATION: The resolution number is assigned by the Bills and Index Division upon filing, just as with bills. “Proposed Resolution” indicates this is a simple resolution of the Parliament expressing its sense or will on a matter. If it were a joint resolution, it would read “Joint Resolution No. .” If concurrent, “Concurrent Resolution No. .” The type of resolution determines its procedural requirements and legal effect.]

A RESOLUTION URGING THE MINISTRY OF BASIC, HIGHER AND TECHNICAL EDUCATION TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT A BANGSAMORO MOTHER TONGUE-BASED MULTILINGUAL EDUCATION PROGRAM THAT INCLUDES MERANAW, MAGUINDANAON, TAUSUG, SAMA, YAKAN, AND OTHER BANGSAMORO LANGUAGES AS MEDIA OF INSTRUCTION IN EARLY CHILDHOOD AND PRIMARY EDUCATION

[ANNOTATION: The title of a resolution serves the same function as the long title of a bill — it describes the full scope of what the resolution covers. Resolution titles typically begin with a gerund or descriptive phrase: “A Resolution Urging…,” “A Resolution Commending…,” “A Resolution Expressing…,” or “A Resolution Directing” The title should be complete enough that a reader understands the resolution’s purpose without reading the body.]

Introduced by MP ___

WHEREAS, the Bangsamoro Organic Law recognizes the right of the Bangsamoro people to preserve and develop their culture and language, and Article V, Section 2(p) expressly grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over “culture and language”;6

[ANNOTATION: The first WHEREAS clause establishes the legal foundation. In resolutions, the WHEREAS clauses follow a deductive order — they move from the broadest, most authoritative basis to the most specific and immediate concern. Starting with the BOL provision grounds the resolution in the Parliament’s legislative authority. Note the exact citation of the BOL provision and the verbatim quotation of the enumerated power.]

WHEREAS, the Republic Act No. 10523, known as the Enhanced Basic Education Act of 2013, mandates the use of the learner’s mother tongue as the primary medium of instruction from Kindergarten to Grade 3, but the implementation of this policy in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region has been limited due to insufficient instructional materials and teacher training in Bangsamoro languages;
