[ANNOTATION: The second WHEREAS clause identifies the national law that applies to the subject and acknowledges the gap between what the law requires and what is actually happening on the ground. This clause narrows from the broad legal authority (first clause) to the specific policy context.]

WHEREAS, the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region is home to thirteen (13) ethnolinguistic groups, and the Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028 identifies the preservation and promotion of Bangsamoro languages as a development priority under its socio-cultural agenda;

[ANNOTATION: The third WHEREAS clause introduces supporting policy context — the BDP — and factual background about the region’s linguistic diversity. Each successive clause gets closer to the specific action the resolution urges. This is the deductive ordering principle in practice: legal authority, then national policy context, then regional development priorities, then the specific problem and proposed response.]

WHEREAS, the development of mother tongue-based instructional materials in Meranaw, Maguindanaon, Tausug, Sama, Yakan, and other Bangsamoro languages requires a coordinated effort between the Ministry, local linguistic experts, cultural institutions, and the communities themselves;

[ANNOTATION: The fourth and final WHEREAS clause identifies the operational challenge and the need for coordination. It sets up the resolution’s operative clause by making clear that the problem requires institutional action, not just goodwill. Four WHEREAS clauses is typical for a resolution of this type. Simple commendatory resolutions may have only two or three; complex policy resolutions may have more.]

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, AS IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED BY THE BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY PARLIAMENT, to urge the Ministry of Basic, Higher and Technical Education to develop and implement, within one (1) year from the adoption of this Resolution, a Bangsamoro Mother Tongue-Based Multilingual Education Program that:

(1) identifies and prioritizes the Bangsamoro languages to be used as media of instruction in early childhood and primary education;

(2) develops culturally appropriate instructional materials in each identified language, in consultation with native speakers, local linguists, and cultural organizations;

(3) establishes a teacher training program for mother tongue-based multilingual instruction; and

(4) coordinates with the Department of Education and the National Commission for Culture and the Arts to ensure alignment with national education standards.

[ANNOTATION: This is the Operative Clause — the heart of the resolution. It states the specific action the Parliament urges, directs, or resolves. The formulation “NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, AS IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED BY THE BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY PARLIAMENT” is standard. The operative clause should be concrete and specific — it names the agency, sets a timeline, and lists what the action should include. Vague resolutions that urge an agency to “look into” a matter carry little weight.]

RESOLVED FURTHER, that the Ministry shall report to the Parliament, through the Committee on Basic, Higher and Technical Education, on the status of implementation of this Resolution within six (6) months from its adoption and every six (6) months thereafter until the program is fully operational.

[ANNOTATION: A RESOLVED FURTHER clause adds a supplementary directive. Here, it creates a reporting mechanism so the Parliament can track whether the Ministry acts on the resolution. This is good practice — a resolution without follow-up is easily ignored. Note that the report goes through the relevant committee, not directly to the plenary. This is consistent with the committee system’s oversight function.]

RESOLVED FINALLY, that copies of this Resolution shall be furnished to the Office of the Chief Minister, the Ministry of Basic, Higher and Technical Education, the Department of Education — Central Office, and the Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority.

[ANNOTATION: The RESOLVED FINALLY clause directs distribution of the adopted resolution to the agencies and offices that need to know about it. This is a standard closing clause in most resolutions. It ensures the resolution reaches the relevant parties and becomes part of the official record.]

Adopted,

(Date of adoption)

[ANNOTATION: The adoption line records the date the Parliament adopted the resolution. This is filled in after the resolution passes, not during drafting.]

(Sgd.)

MP ___ Author

[ANNOTATION: The author signature block identifies who filed the resolution. If there are co-authors, each signs. The signature block typically appears at the end. In some formats, the author’s full name and title are printed below the signature line.]

Appendix D: Pre-Drafting Checklist

This checklist is designed to be used before you start writing. Go through it after receiving a legislative directive and completing your research, but before you draft the first section of the bill. If you cannot check off an item, stop and address it. A gap at this stage becomes a structural problem in the draft.

Print this checklist. Keep it on your desk. Use it every time.

1. Legislative Directive

  • ☐ 1.1 Has the MP provided a clear legislative direction?
  • ☐ 1.2 Is the Initial Legislative Template documented?
  • ☐ 1.3 Does the proposal align with the MP’s legislative agenda?
  • ☐ 1.4 Has the MP approved the Initial Legislative Template?
  • ☐ 1.5 Has the MP identified the policy objectives the bill must achieve?
  • ☐ 1.6 Has the MP indicated a desired timeline for filing?
  • ☐ 1.7 Have potential co-authors been identified or discussed?

2. Legal Authority

  • ☐ 2.1 Is the subject within the enumerated powers (BOL Article V, Section 2)?
  • ☐ 2.2 If relying on the General Welfare Clause (Article V, Section 3), has the legal basis been documented and the connection to an express power articulated?
  • ☐ 2.3 Does the 1987 Constitution permit this legislation?
  • ☐ 2.4 Are there national laws that apply to or preempt the subject?
  • ☐ 2.5 If national laws exist, has the relationship between the proposed BAA and those laws been analyzed?
  • ☐ 2.6 Are there existing Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts on the same or a related subject?
  • ☐ 2.7 If existing BAAs apply, has the proposed bill been checked for conflicts or overlaps?
  • ☐ 2.8 Are there BOL provisions that specifically address the subject? If so, have they been quoted and cited?
  • ☐ 2.9 Has the residual clause been considered — is there any aspect of this subject reserved to Congress?
  • ☐ 2.10 Are there Shari’ah considerations relevant to the subject matter?

3. Research

  • ☐ 3.1 Has the Policy Research and Legal Services (PRLS) been consulted?
  • ☐ 3.2 Have all related national laws and their implementing rules and regulations been identified?
  • ☐ 3.3 Have all related BAAs, resolutions, and executive issuances been identified?
  • ☐ 3.4 Has a comparative review been conducted — how do other jurisdictions or regions handle this subject?
  • ☐ 3.5 Is the Legislative Research Briefer complete?
  • ☐ 3.6 Has the Legislative Research Briefer been reviewed and approved by the MP?
  • ☐ 3.7 Have relevant data and statistics been gathered (enrollment figures, budget data, population data, incidence rates, etc.)?
  • ☐ 3.8 Have relevant provisions of the Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP) 2023-2028 been reviewed for alignment?
  • ☐ 3.9 Have implementing rules and regulations of related laws been reviewed for practical insights?

4. Consultation

  • ☐ 4.1 Have subject-matter experts been consulted?
  • ☐ 4.2 Have the implementing agencies that will carry out the law been consulted?
  • ☐ 4.3 Have the implementing agencies confirmed their capacity to implement the bill’s requirements?
  • ☐ 4.4 Have concerned sectors and constituencies been consulted?
  • ☐ 4.5 Have affected local government units been consulted?
  • ☐ 4.6 Has the gender impact of the proposed legislation been assessed?
  • ☐ 4.7 Has the human rights impact been assessed?
  • ☐ 4.8 If the bill affects indigenous peoples, have their representatives been consulted consistent with their customary processes?
  • ☐ 4.9 Have consultation findings been documented and shared with the MP?

5. Drafting Readiness

  • ☐ 5.1 Is the working outline prepared?
  • ☐ 5.2 Has the working outline been reviewed by the MP or a senior staff member?
  • ☐ 5.3 Has the comparative matrix been prepared (showing how the proposed bill relates to existing laws)?
  • ☐ 5.4 Are all relevant BOL provisions identified, quoted, and ready for citation?
  • ☐ 5.5 Has the bill’s structure been determined (number of articles/chapters, if any, and the general sequence of sections)?
  • ☐ 5.6 Have the terms that require definition been listed?
  • ☐ 5.7 Has the implementing agency or mechanism been identified?
  • ☐ 5.8 Has the funding source or appropriation mechanism been determined?
  • ☐ 5.9 Has the penalty or enforcement scheme been determined?
  • ☐ 5.10 Has a timeline for drafting been set, with milestones for first draft, MP review, and finalization?
  • ☐ 5.11 Are all source materials organized and accessible for reference during drafting?

6. Compliance and Consistency

  • ☐ 6.1 Has the proposed bill been checked against the BOL compliance requirements (Chapter 11)?
  • ☐ 6.2 Is the terminology consistent with the BOL and existing BAAs?
  • ☐ 6.3 Has the bill been checked for potential conflicts with the Constitution’s bill of rights and other provisions?
  • ☐ 6.4 If the bill creates a government body, does it specify composition, powers, functions, funding, and accountability?
  • ☐ 6.5 If the bill imposes obligations, does every obligation have an accountable body and a consequence for non-compliance?
  • ☐ 6.6 If the bill requires expenditure, does it include an appropriation clause?

How to use this checklist. Items 1 through 4 should be completed before drafting begins. Items 5 and 6 should be verified at the start of drafting and re-checked when the first draft is complete. If you discover during drafting that an item in sections 1 through 4 was not adequately addressed, pause the draft and go back. It is faster to fill a research gap now than to restructure a bill later.

This checklist does not replace the Quality Review Checklist (Appendix E), which is used after the draft is written. The Pre-Drafting Checklist ensures you are ready to write. The Quality Review Checklist ensures what you wrote is sound.

Appendix F: Standard Clause Templates

This appendix provides the prescribed verbatim text for standard clauses used in Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts. These templates are derived from pattern analysis of all 89 enacted BAAs (BAA 1-89) and represent the most widely used and recommended formulations.7

Use these templates as your starting point. Copy the exact wording unless the bill’s subject matter requires a specific variation. Consistency in standard clauses reduces drafting errors, speeds committee review, and avoids unnecessary interpellation questions about clause wording.

F.1 Separability Clause

Recommended Standard (based on BAA 25, 26, 30 — used in general legislation):

If any provision of this Act or the application of such provision is declared invalid, the remainder of this Act shall not be affected by such declaration.

Variant for Municipality Creation Bills (BAA 41-48 — includes BOL reference):

If, for any cause, any part of this Act is declared unconstitutional or contrary to the provisions of Republic Act No. 11054 or the Bangsamoro Organic Law, the rest of the provisions hereof which are not affected shall remain in full force and effect.

Variant for Hospital Bills (BAA 78, 79 — “declared separable” formulation):

The provisions of this Act are hereby declared separable, and in the event one or more such provisions or parts thereof are declared unconstitutional, such declaration of unconstitutionality shall not affect the validity of the other provisions thereof.

F.2 General Repealing Clause

Recommended Standard (based on BAA 73 — comprehensive modern formulation):

All laws, decrees, executive orders, issuances, rules, and regulations, or parts thereof, that are inconsistent with the provisions of this Act are hereby repealed, amended, or modified accordingly.

Variant for Municipality Creation Bills (BAA 41-48):

All laws, decrees, executive orders, or regulations or any part thereof which may be contrary or inconsistent to this Act are hereby repealed, amended, modified or altered accordingly.

Variant with BOL Exception (BAA 53-55, 70-71 — later municipality creation bills):

All laws, decrees, executive orders or regulations or any part thereof which may be contrary or inconsistent to this Act, except those that declare any part of this Act unconstitutional or contrary to the provisions of the Republic Act No. 11054, are hereby repealed, amended, modified, or altered accordingly.

F.3 Specific Repealing Clause (Fill-in-the-Blank)

When the bill repeals identified provisions of existing law, use this pattern before the general repealing clause:

[Section/s ___ of Bangsamoro Autonomy Act No. ___, otherwise known as the “__________,” is/are hereby repealed.]

All laws, decrees, executive orders, issuances, rules, and regulations, or parts thereof, that are inconsistent with the provisions of this Act are hereby repealed, amended, or modified accordingly.

Source: Pattern derived from BAA 39 and BAA 50, which include specific named repeals. A specific repeal is always stronger than a general one. Courts disfavor implied repeals — if you intend to repeal something, name it explicitly.

F.4 Effectivity Clause

Recommended Standard (based on BAA 41-48, 53-55, 70-71 — Bangsamoro Gazette option, used in 13+ BAAs):

This Act shall take effect after fifteen (15) days from its publication in the Bangsamoro Gazette or in one (1) newspaper of regional circulation.

Variant — Newspaper Only (BAA 1, 2, 11, 36 — earlier BAAs):

This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days after its publication in a newspaper of regional circulation in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao.

Variant — Official Gazette Option (BAA 76 — 2025 formulation):

This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days after its publication in the Official Gazette or in a newspaper of regional circulation in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao.

Variant — Complete Publication (BAA 73):

This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days following its complete publication in a newspaper of regional circulation.

F.5 IRR Clause — 60-Day Variant

Standard 60-Day (based on BAA 25 — most common timeframe for hospital and regulatory bills):

The [implementing agency] shall promulgate the necessary implementing rules and regulations within sixty (60) days after the effectivity of this Act.

60-Day with Consultation (BAA 73 — recommended for bills with LGU involvement):

Within sixty (60) days from the effectivity of this Act, the [implementing agency] shall promulgate the necessary rules and regulations, in consultation with [stakeholders], for the effective implementation of this Act.

60-Day with Dual Agency (BAA 80):

Within sixty (60) days from the effectivity of this Act, the [primary agency] and [secondary agency] shall promulgate the appropriate Implementing Rules and Regulations for the effective implementation of this Act.

F.6 IRR Clause — 90-Day Variant

Standard 90-Day (based on BAA 78, 79 — used for newer hospital bills):

The [implementing agency] shall promulgate the necessary rules and regulations to implement the provisions of this Act within ninety (90) days from its effectivity.

F.7 Suppletory Clause

Standard Formulation (based on BAA 82, Section 275 — Bangsamoro Labor and Employment Code):

In cases where this Act contains no applicable provision concerning [subject matter], the [national law] and other relevant [subject-area] legislation shall be applied suppletorily: Provided, That they are not contrary to existing Bangsamoro laws.

Source: BAA 82 (Bangsamoro Labor and Employment Code of 2024). Use this clause in bills that regulate a subject where comprehensive national legislation already exists and where gaps in the Bangsamoro law should be filled by the national law rather than creating a vacuum.

F.8 Mandatory Review Clause

Recommended Formulation (based on best practice — no single BAA uses this exact text, but elements appear in sunset/reporting provisions of BAA 78):

Within [three (3) / five (5)] years from the effectivity of this Act, the [implementing agency] shall submit to the Parliament, through the [relevant committee], a comprehensive evaluation report on the implementation and impact of this Act. The Parliament shall review this Act based on the evaluation report and determine whether amendments, extension, or repeal are warranted.

Source: Derived from the annual reporting and sunset provisions in BAA 78 (Tipo-Tipo General Hospital) and BAA 73 (Iranon Provincial Hospital). Although no enacted BAA uses a standalone “Mandatory Review Clause” by that name, the principle is embedded in the reporting requirements of newer legislation.

Appendix G: Hospital Bill Template

This template is derived from the 16 hospital BAAs enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament (BAA 25-30, 66-69, 73-74, 76, 78-80).8 It follows the structure of the most recent and complete versions — BAA 78 (Tipo-Tipo General Hospital, 2025) and BAA 73 (Iranon Provincial Hospital, 2023) — which represent the Parliament’s current drafting standard for hospital legislation.

Fill in the blanks marked with [___]. Delete optional sections not applicable to the bill. Annotations in brackets explain drafting choices.

P.B. No. ___

Republic of the Philippines Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY PARLIAMENT Bangsamoro Government Center, Cotabato City

___ REGULAR SESSION

BANGSAMORO AUTONOMY ACT NO. ___

Begun and held in Cotabato City, on Thursday, the 15th day of September 2022.

[Choose one of the following long title patterns:]

For Establishing a New Hospital:

AN ACT ESTABLISHING A LEVEL [I/II/III] [DISTRICT/GENERAL/PROVINCIAL] HOSPITAL IN THE [MUNICIPALITY/CITY/PROVINCE] OF [___], PROVINCE OF [___], AND APPROPRIATING FUNDS THEREFOR

For Upgrading/Converting an Existing Hospital:

AN ACT [UPGRADING/CONVERTING] THE [current hospital name] IN THE [MUNICIPALITY/CITY] OF [___], PROVINCE OF [___] [INTO A (target level) HOSPITAL / FROM A (current capacity)-BED CAPACITY TO A (target capacity)-BED CAPACITY] [PROVINCIAL/GENERAL] HOSPITAL TO BE KNOWN AS THE [new hospital name], AND APPROPRIATING FUNDS THEREFOR

Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority in Parliament assembled:

SECTION 1. Short Title. – This Act shall be known as the “[Hospital Name] Act of [Year].“

[ANNOTATION: Short title is optional for hospital bills — approximately half of the 16 hospital BAAs omit it. Include it for named institutions.]

SEC. 2. Declaration of Policy. – It is a policy of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM) as provided under Section 22, Article IX of Republic Act No. 11054,9 otherwise known as the “Organic Law for the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao” to provide for a comprehensive and integrated health service delivery for its constituents and establish by law a general hospital system to serve the health requirements of its people and ensure that the individual’s basic right to life shall be attainable through the prompt intervention of excellent and affordable medical services.

[ANNOTATION: This declaration of policy is used verbatim across 8+ hospital BAAs. Use this exact text unless the bill addresses a unique health concern requiring additional policy language. Note: The BOL source text (Section 22, Article IX) reads “individual basic right to life” without an apostrophe-s, but the enacted hospital BAAs consistently use “individual’s basic right to life.” Follow the enacted BAA wording shown here, which is the established legislative template.]

SEC. 3. [Creation / Conversion / Upgrading] of [Hospital Name]. –

[For Establishing:] There shall be established a Level [___] [district/general/provincial] hospital with a [___]-bed capacity in the [Municipality/City] of [___], Province of [___], to be known as the “[Hospital Name].“

[For Converting:] The [current hospital name] in the [Municipality/City] of [___], Province of [___], is hereby converted into a [___]-bed capacity [provincial/general] hospital, to be known as the [new hospital name], under the direct supervision and control of the Ministry of Health (MOH).

[For Upgrading:] The [current hospital name] in the [Municipality/City] of [___], Province of [___], is hereby upgraded from a [current capacity]-bed capacity to a [target capacity]-bed capacity [level] hospital.

SEC. 4. Administration and Supervision. – The [Hospital Name] shall be under the administrative supervision and operational control of the Ministry of Health (MOH), which is hereby authorized to determine and approve the organizational structure necessary for the effective and efficient management and operation of the hospital.

The MOH shall formulate the necessary guidelines for its operations as a Level [___] hospital and ensure funding for the construction of the hospital building and other complementary infrastructure, facilities, and medical equipment, as well as the initial costs for maintenance and personnel services.

SEC. 5. Submission of Annual Development Plan. – The Chief of Hospital, in consultation and coordination with the [___ Province] Integrated Provincial Health Office, shall submit to the MOH an annual development plan reflecting the developmental goals of the hospital. The medical personnel, capital outlays, and budgetary needs for the operation of the hospital shall be based on the annual hospital development plan.

Any future increase in bed capacity and upgrading of healthcare services and facilities shall be consistent with the annual hospital development plan.

SEC. 6. Integration of the Universal Health Care Program. – All relevant and pertinent provisions of Republic Act No. 11223, otherwise known as the “Universal Health Care Act,” shall be integrated into the operational system of the [Hospital Name].

SEC. 7. [OPTIONAL] Transfer of Management and Control to the Provincial Government. – The full management and control of the [Hospital Name] shall be transferred from the Bangsamoro Government to the Provincial Government of [___] upon the occurrence of either of the following, whichever comes first:

a) A formal request from the Provincial Government, with the concurrence of the MOH confirming the local government unit’s readiness and institutional capacity to manage the hospital; or

b) A favorable assessment conducted by the MOH, every five (5) years, in accordance with its prescribed guidelines and standards, determining that the Provincial Government possesses sufficient administrative, technical, and financial capability to independently operate the hospital.

Upon determination of either condition, the MOH shall issue a formal order of transfer, which shall take effect upon completion of a joint transition plan to be developed by the MOH and the Provincial Government. This plan shall outline the phased turnover of responsibilities, personnel, assets, and systems, and shall include a clear timetable for implementation not exceeding one (1) year from the issuance of the transfer order.

[ANNOTATION: This section appears in BAA 78 and 79 (2025 hospital bills). Include it when the long-term plan is for provincial management. Omit for hospitals that will remain under direct MOH supervision.]

SEC. 8. [OPTIONAL] Continuing Support. – Notwithstanding the transfer of full management and control to the Provincial Government, the Bangsamoro Government, through the MOH, shall continue to provide technical support and, when necessary, financial support for the operation and administration of the hospital.

The MOH shall submit an annual report to the Bangsamoro Parliament, through the Committee on Health and the Committee on Finance, Budget, and Management, detailing the nature, scope, and impact of such support.

SEC. 9. Appropriation. – An initial amount of [___ Pesos (PHP___.00)], necessary to carry out the provisions of this Act, shall be charged against the [year] General Appropriations Act of the Bangsamoro. Thereafter, the funds required for the continued operation and maintenance of the hospital shall be included in the annual appropriations of the Bangsamoro Government.

[ANNOTATION: The 2025 hospital BAAs (78, 79, 80) specify PhP 50,000,000.00 as the initial amount. Adjust based on the hospital’s level and scope.]

SEC. 10. Implementing Rules and Regulations. – The MOH shall promulgate the necessary rules and regulations to implement the provisions of this Act within [sixty (60) / ninety (90)] days from its effectivity.

[ANNOTATION: BAA 25, 26, 30, 73, 80 use 60 days. BAA 78, 79 use 90 days. Choose based on the complexity of implementation requirements.]

SEC. 11. Annual Report. – The MOH shall submit to the Chief Minister and to the Parliament, through the Committee on Health and the Committee on Finance, Budget, and Management, an annual report on the outcome and progress of the [creation/conversion/upgrading].

SEC. 12. Separability Clause. – The provisions of this Act are hereby declared separable, and in the event one or more such provisions or parts thereof are declared unconstitutional, such declaration of unconstitutionality shall not affect the validity of the other provisions thereof.

SEC. 13. Repealing Clause. – All laws, decrees, executive orders, issuances, rules, and regulations, or parts thereof, that are inconsistent with the provisions of this Act are hereby repealed, amended, or modified accordingly.

SEC. 14. Effectivity. – This Act shall take effect fifteen (15) days after its publication in the Official Gazette or in a newspaper of regional circulation in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao.

Appendix H: Municipality Creation Bill Template

This template is derived from all 11 municipality creation BAAs enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament (BAA 41-48, 53-55).10 These bills follow a highly standardized structure — 11 sections in a fixed order — making them the most templatable bill type in the Bangsamoro legislative corpus.

Fill in the blanks marked with [___]. The core text is taken verbatim from BAA 41 (Municipality of Pahamuddin) and BAA 43 (Municipality of Nabalawag), with variations from BAA 53-55 noted where applicable.

P.B. No. ___

Republic of the Philippines Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao BANGSAMORO TRANSITION AUTHORITY PARLIAMENT Bangsamoro Government Center, Cotabato City

___ REGULAR SESSION

BANGSAMORO AUTONOMY ACT NO. ___

Begun and held in Cotabato City, on Thursday, the 15th day of September 2022.

AN ACT CREATING THE MUNICIPALITY OF [___], APPROPRIATING FUNDS THEREFOR, AND FOR OTHER PURPOSES

Be it enacted by the Bangsamoro Transition Authority in Parliament assembled:

SECTION 1. Declaration of Policy. – In the exercise of genuine autonomy and self-governance, the Bangsamoro Government is empowered to create, divide, merge, abolish, or substantially alter boundaries of municipalities or barangays in accordance with a law enacted by the Parliament. The municipalities or barangays created, divided, merged, or whose boundaries are substantially altered, shall be entitled to their appropriate share in the National Tax Allotment: Provided, That it shall be approved by a majority of the votes cast in a plebiscite in the political units directly affected.

[ANNOTATION: This declaration of policy is used verbatim across all 11 municipality creation BAAs. Do not alter it. Note: BAA 53-55 use “Internal Revenue Allotment” instead of “National Tax Allotment” — use whichever term is current at time of drafting.]

SEC. 2. Creation of the Municipality. – The Barangays of [list all barangays in sequence, separated by commas, with “and” before the last], [all belonging to / belonging to] the Municipality of [mother municipality/municipalities], [Province of ___ / North Cotabato], which petitioned and voted to be included in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao in the 2019 plebiscite, are hereby separated from their mother [municipality/municipalities]. As such, said barangays are constituted into a distinct and independent municipality to be known as the Municipality of [___], Special Geographic Area of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao.

[ANNOTATION: If barangays come from more than one mother municipality (as in BAA 43 — Nabalawag, drawn from both Midsayap and Aleosan), list each group separately and name both mother municipalities. The “Special Geographic Area” designation applies to municipalities created from the 63 barangays that voted for inclusion in BARMM in the 2019 plebiscite.]

SEC. 3. Seat of Government. – The seat of government of the new Municipality of [___] shall be established in Barangay [___].

SEC. 4. Territorial Boundary. – The territorial boundary of the new Municipality of [___] shall follow the existing boundaries of the constituent barangays as above enumerated.

[ANNOTATION: Attach a map and technical description as an appendix — see BAA 41 and 43 for the required format, including a political boundary map, certification by a Geodetic Engineer, and a technical description table with lines, bearings, and distances.]

SEC. 5. Plebiscite Requirement. – The Municipality of [___] shall acquire corporate existence upon ratification of its creation by a majority of the votes cast by qualified voters in a plebiscite to be conducted in the barangays comprising the municipality pursuant to Section 2 hereof within sixty (60) days after the approval of the Act.

SEC. 6. Conduct of Plebiscite. – The Commission on Elections, through the Bangsamoro Electoral Office, shall conduct and supervise the plebiscite. The expenses in holding the plebiscite shall be taken out of the current fiscal year appropriations of the Bangsamoro Government, subject to usual accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

SEC. 7. Appointment of Municipal Officials. – The first mayor, vice-mayor, and the eight (8) members of the Sangguniang Bayan of the new municipality shall be appointed by the Chief Minister of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao with due regard to representation of women of the Bangsamoro. They shall continue to hold office until their successors shall have been elected in the next general elections for local officials, and shall have qualified: Provided, That the incumbent elective members of the Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality of [mother municipality/municipalities] who are actual residents of the new municipality shall serve the remaining terms of their elective offices in [said mother municipality / their aforementioned mother municipalities].

[ANNOTATION: If barangays come from multiple mother municipalities, adjust the proviso to reference all mother municipalities — see BAA 43 for the multi-municipality formulation.]

SEC. 8. Financial Assistance Pending Entitlement to [National Tax Allotment / Internal Revenue Allotment]. – The Bangsamoro Government shall appropriate and provide financial assistance for the administration and operation of the Municipal Government of [___] every month until such time as the new municipality shall have been allocated its share in the [national tax allotment / internal revenue allotment], subject to usual accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

[ANNOTATION: BAA 53-55 add a minimum amount: “…an amount of not less than Two Million Five Hundred Thousand Pesos (PhP2,500,000.00) per month…” Consider including a minimum amount if precedent supports it.]

SEC. 9. Separability Clause. – If, for any cause, any part of this Act is declared unconstitutional or contrary to the provisions of Republic Act No. 11054 or the Bangsamoro Organic Law, the rest of the provisions hereof which are not affected shall remain in full force and effect.

SEC. 10. Repealing Clause. – All laws, decrees, executive orders, or regulations or any part thereof which may be contrary or inconsistent to this Act are hereby repealed, amended, modified or altered accordingly.

[ANNOTATION: BAA 53-55 add a BOL exception clause: “…except those that declare any part of this Act unconstitutional or contrary to the provisions of the Republic Act No. 11054…” Consider using the expanded version.]

SEC. 11. Effectivity. – This Act shall take effect after fifteen (15) days from its publication in the Bangsamoro Gazette or in one (1) newspaper of regional circulation.

Required Appendix: Map and Technical Description

Every municipality creation bill must include as an appendix:

  1. Political Boundary Map — showing the municipality’s boundaries in relation to adjacent LGUs, with the following header information:
  2. Municipality name
  3. Province (if applicable)
  4. Island: Mindanao
  5. Total area in hectares

  6. Scale and datum reference

  7. Certification — signed by a Geodetic Engineer certifying the territorial boundary, with recommending approval from the Chief of the Regional Surveys Division and approval from the Ministry of the Interior and Local Government (or relevant authority)

  8. Technical Description — a table listing all boundary lines with bearings and distances, tie line reference, total area, and meets and bounds description

Source: All 11 municipality creation BAAs include this appendix. See BAA 41 (Pahamuddin) and BAA 43 (Nabalawag) for complete examples.

  1. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 2, art. V. ↩

  2. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 3, art. V. ↩

  3. Analysis of 89 enacted BAAs, 2019-2026. ↩

  4. Res. No. 268, BTA Parliament, Rule XVIII, sec. 2(g). ↩

  5. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 2(kk) and sec. 2(v), art. V. ↩

  6. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 2(p), art. V. ↩

  7. Id., analysis of 89 enacted BAAs, 2019-2026. ↩

  8. Id. ↩

  9. Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 22, art. IX. ↩

  10. Id., analysis of 89 enacted BAAs, 2019-2026; Rep. Act No. 11054, sec. 2(l), art. V. ↩

Back to Table of Contents
Appendices

Appendix E — Quality Review Checklist (Detachable)

This checklist is designed to be printed and used after completing a bill draft, before submitting it for review. Work through every item. If you check “No” or cannot answer confidently, stop and fix the problem before proceeding.

The checklist is the companion to Appendix D (Pre-Drafting Checklist). Appendix D helps you prepare before writing. This appendix helps you verify after writing.

Print this. Keep a copy on your desk. Use it every time.

I. Legal Authority

No.ItemYesNoN/A
1 Every provision falls within the BOL enumerated powers (Article V, Section 2) or the general welfare clause (Article V, Section 3). ☐ ☐ ☐
2 No provision conflicts with the 1987 Philippine Constitution. ☐ ☐ ☐
3 No provision conflicts with the Bangsamoro Organic Law (Republic Act No. 11054). ☐ ☐ ☐
4 Applicable national laws on the same subject have been identified and reviewed for conflicts. ☐ ☐ ☐
5 Existing Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts on the same subject have been reviewed for conflicts, overlaps, and gaps. ☐ ☐ ☐
6 No provision intrudes on powers reserved to Congress under the residual clause. ☐ ☐ ☐
7 Shari’ah considerations have been addressed where the bill affects personal, family, or property relations involving Muslims. ☐ ☐ ☐
8 Human rights implications have been assessed against Article III of the BOL and the Philippine Bill of Rights. ☐ ☐ ☐
9 If the bill imposes taxes or fees, it complies with the fiscal provisions of BOL Article XII. ☐ ☐ ☐
10 If the bill creates a government body, it complies with BOL provisions on bureaucratic structure. ☐ ☐ ☐
11 If the bill exercises concurrent jurisdiction with national government, the boundaries of authority are clearly stated. ☐ ☐ ☐
12 Relevant Supreme Court decisions affecting the bill’s subject matter have been checked. ☐ ☐ ☐
13 The bill does not delegate legislative power to the executive beyond what the BOL permits. ☐ ☐ ☐
14 If the bill touches on indigenous peoples’ rights within the Bangsamoro, Article IX, Section 3 of the BOL has been respected (rights under RA 8371 “shall not be diminished”). ☐ ☐ ☐

Notes on Legal Authority:

 

 

II. Structure and Completeness

No.ItemYesNoN/A
15 The long title accurately describes the bill’s content and scope. ☐ ☐ ☐
16 The one-subject, one-title rule is satisfied — the bill does not contain riders or unrelated provisions. ☐ ☐ ☐
17 The enacting clause uses the correct standard formulation for the current period. ☐ ☐ ☐
18 A short title is present in the preliminary provisions. ☐ ☐ ☐
19 A declaration of policy states the bill’s purpose, objectives, and legislative intent. ☐ ☐ ☐
20 All terms used in a technical, specialized, or restricted sense are defined in the definition of terms section. ☐ ☐ ☐
21 The definitions section does not define words in their ordinary meaning. ☐ ☐ ☐
22 Scope and coverage are clearly stated — who and what the bill applies to. ☐ ☐ ☐
23 Substantive provisions are organized in a logical sequence — general to specific, rights to obligations, prohibitions to penalties. ☐ ☐ ☐
24 Every duty or obligation identifies an accountable body or person. ☐ ☐ ☐
25 Every right identifies who may exercise it and who must respect it. ☐ ☐ ☐
26 Every prohibition has an enforcement mechanism — a penalty, sanction, or consequence. ☐ ☐ ☐
27 Every penalty corresponds to a clearly stated prohibition in the substantive provisions. ☐ ☐ ☐
28 The implementing agency or agencies are identified. ☐ ☐ ☐
29 The implementing agency has the capacity — or the bill provides for the capacity — to carry out the mandate. ☐ ☐ ☐
30 An IRR provision is included if the bill requires detailed rulemaking for implementation. ☐ ☐ ☐
31 The IRR provision includes a specific deadline for promulgation (not “within a reasonable time”). ☐ ☐ ☐
32 An appropriation or funding provision is included if the bill requires funding. ☐ ☐ ☐
33 The funding source is identified — annual BEP, special fund, or specific appropriation. ☐ ☐ ☐
34 A separability clause is present. ☐ ☐ ☐
35 A repealing clause identifies specific provisions of existing law that are repealed or modified. ☐ ☐ ☐
36 The repealing clause does not rely solely on a general repealing clause without specific repeals. ☐ ☐ ☐
37 An effectivity clause is present, following the standard formulation (fifteen days after publication). ☐ ☐ ☐
38 Transitory provisions address existing conditions — pending applications, current license holders, ongoing contracts — if applicable. ☐ ☐ ☐
39 If the bill creates a board or body, the composition, qualifications, terms, quorum, and voting requirements are all specified and internally consistent. ☐ ☐ ☐
40 If the bill creates a pilot or temporary program, a sunset clause with an evaluation mechanism is included. ☐ ☐ ☐
41 Reporting and oversight requirements are specified — who reports, to whom, how often, in what format. ☐ ☐ ☐
42 An explanatory note accompanies the bill (de facto required in the Bangsamoro Parliament). ☐ ☐ ☐

Notes on Structure and Completeness:

 

 

III. Language and Style

No.ItemYesNoN/A
43 “Shall” is used only for mandatory duties and obligations. ☐ ☐ ☐
44 “May” is used only for permissive authority or discretion. ☐ ☐ ☐
45 “Must” is used only for conditions or prerequisites. ☐ ☐ ☐
46 “Should,” “will,” “would,” and “ought” are not used for obligations or conditions. ☐ ☐ ☐
47 Active voice is used throughout — every obligation identifies who acts. ☐ ☐ ☐
48 Present tense is used for normative provisions (not future tense). ☐ ☐ ☐
49 No archaic legalisms appear — no “hereby,” “henceforth,” “hereinbefore,” “hereinafter,” “aforementioned,” “heretofore.” ☐ ☐ ☐
50 No redundant doublets appear — no “null and void,” “cease and desist,” “each and every,” “due and payable,” “fit and proper.” ☐ ☐ ☐
51 “And/or” does not appear anywhere in the bill. ☐ ☐ ☐
52 Lists use proper conjunctive (“and”) or disjunctive (“or”) format, with the conjunction placed before the final item. ☐ ☐ ☐
53 Each sentence expresses one idea. No sentence exceeds four lines of text. ☐ ☐ ☐
54 Long provisions are broken into numbered sub-items or lettered paragraphs for readability. ☐ ☐ ☐
55 Definitions use “means” for exhaustive definitions and “includes” for non-exhaustive definitions — not interchangeably. ☐ ☐ ☐
56 Every defined term passes the substitution test — the definition can replace the term in every instance without altering the provision’s meaning. ☐ ☐ ☐
57 Numbers are written as words with figures in parentheses — “thirty (30) days.” ☐ ☐ ☐
58 Monetary amounts are written in words and figures with the PhP abbreviation — “Two Hundred Thousand Pesos (PhP200,000.00).” ☐ ☐ ☐
59 No vague phrases appear without further specificity — no “as may be necessary,” “within a reasonable time,” “to the extent practicable,” “as appropriate.” ☐ ☐ ☐
60 Gender-neutral or gender-inclusive language is used throughout — “chairperson” not “chairman,” “fisherfolk” not “fishermen.” ☐ ☐ ☐
61 If pronouns are used, they are inclusive — “he or she,” “his or her” — or the provision is restructured to avoid pronouns. ☐ ☐ ☐
62 Section headings are present, descriptive, and formatted consistently (italicized for enrolled bills). ☐ ☐ ☐
63 Sections use “SEC.” followed by Arabic numerals. Articles use Roman numerals. ☐ ☐ ☐
64 Legal citations follow the established format — BOL provisions, national laws, BAAs, and resolutions are cited correctly. ☐ ☐ ☐
65 Abbreviations and acronyms are defined at first use and used consistently thereafter. ☐ ☐ ☐

Notes on Language and Style:

 

 

IV. Internal Consistency

No.ItemYesNoN/A
66 The same term is used for the same concept throughout the bill — no synonyms, no alternating names. ☐ ☐ ☐
67 Section numbering is sequential with no gaps and no duplicates. ☐ ☐ ☐
68 Every cross-reference points to the correct section — each has been individually verified. ☐ ☐ ☐
69 Defined terms are used exactly as defined — no variation in form, no partial use. ☐ ☐ ☐
70 No contradictions exist between any two provisions in the bill. ☐ ☐ ☐
71 If the bill quotes the BOL or another law, the quotation is verbatim. ☐ ☐ ☐
72 Timelines are internally consistent — no provision gives a different deadline for the same action. ☐ ☐ ☐
73 Qualifications for any office or position are stated consistently across all provisions that reference them. ☐ ☐ ☐
74 The scope in the declaration of policy matches the scope in the substantive provisions. ☐ ☐ ☐
75 If the bill lists members of a body in the composition section, the quorum and voting provisions are mathematically consistent with the stated number. ☐ ☐ ☐
76 The bill’s table of contents (if included) matches the actual section headings and numbering in the body. ☐ ☐ ☐
77 Penalty provisions cover every prohibited act — no prohibition is missing a penalty, no penalty references a non-existent prohibition. ☐ ☐ ☐
78 The long title is still accurate after all revisions — it reflects the bill as it stands now, not an earlier draft. ☐ ☐ ☐

Notes on Internal Consistency:

 

 

V. The Interpellation Test

No.ItemYesNoN/A
79 You have read the bill as a skeptical opposition MP and identified every weakness. ☐ ☐ ☐
80 Every provision can be implemented as written — no provision assumes resources, capacity, or conditions that do not exist. ☐ ☐ ☐
81 You can answer the question “what problem does this bill solve?” with specificity, not generalities. ☐ ☐ ☐
82 You can defend every policy choice in the bill — every threshold, every deadline, every penalty amount. ☐ ☐ ☐
83 You have identified all gaps — scenarios the bill should cover but does not. ☐ ☐ ☐
84 You have identified all unintended consequences — ways the bill could be misused, misinterpreted, or produce results contrary to its intent. ☐ ☐ ☐
85 The bill does not create an unfunded mandate — every financial obligation has a funding source. ☐ ☐ ☐
86 The bill does not delegate unlimited discretion to any single person or agency. ☐ ☐ ☐
87 The bill can be explained in plain language to a non-lawyer — a constituent, a journalist, an implementing officer — without confusion. ☐ ☐ ☐
88 At least one other person — a colleague, a PRLS researcher, a subject-matter expert — has reviewed the draft and provided feedback. ☐ ☐ ☐

Notes on the Interpellation Test:

 

 

VI. Specialized Reviews

No.ItemYesNoN/A
89 Gender sensitivity review completed — gender-neutral language, women’s representation provisions, and sex-disaggregated data requirements addressed (see Chapter 11, Section 11.4). ☐ ☐ ☐
90 Human rights review completed — the bill does not violate any right in Article III of the BOL, the Philippine Bill of Rights, or applicable international instruments (see Chapter 11, Section 11.5). ☐ ☐ ☐
91 BOL compliance review completed — the bill has been checked provision-by-provision against the relevant articles of the BOL (see Chapter 11, Section 11.6). ☐ ☐ ☐
92 Penalty provisions are proportionate, specific, and include due process protections. ☐ ☐ ☐
93 Administrative proceedings in the bill provide for notice, hearing, right to counsel, written decision, and appeal. ☐ ☐ ☐
94 If the bill affects indigenous peoples within the Bangsamoro, their rights under the BOL and UNDRIP have been considered. ☐ ☐ ☐
95 The bill aligns with the Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028 — the relevant development goals and strategies have been identified. ☐ ☐ ☐
96 If the bill requires interagency coordination, a coordination mechanism is specified. ☐ ☐ ☐

Notes on Specialized Reviews:

 

 

VII. Final Verification

No.ItemYesNoN/A
97 The explanatory note is consistent with the bill as drafted — it describes the current version, not an earlier draft. ☐ ☐ ☐
98 All cross-references have been verified one final time after all revisions. ☐ ☐ ☐
99 Section numbering has been verified one final time after all revisions. ☐ ☐ ☐
100 The bill has been proofread for typographical errors, misspellings, and formatting inconsistencies. ☐ ☐ ☐
101 The comparative matrix (if prepared) has been updated to reflect the final version. ☐ ☐ ☐
102 The correct and final version is the one that will be filed with the Secretary-General’s office. ☐ ☐ ☐

Review Sign-Off
Drafter:
Date of Review:
Items Flagged for Correction:
Corrections Completed (Date):
Reviewed by (Second Reader):
Date:

This checklist is designed to be reproduced and used as a standalone document. Permission is granted to photocopy or print this appendix for use in legislative offices of the Bangsamoro Parliament.

—

Appendix F — Legislative Research Briefer Template

This template follows the Completed Staff Work (CSW) framework described in Chapter 8. It is designed to be filled in for each legislative measure that requires analysis. Print the template, fill it in by hand or digitally, and attach supporting documents.

The template covers every section of the legislative briefer. For detailed guidance on how to complete each section, refer to the corresponding sections in Chapter 8.

LEGISLATIVE RESEARCH BRIEFER

Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament

Control Information
Control No.: LB--
Date Prepared:
Prepared by:
Designation:
Office of MP:
Version: ☐ Initial   ☐ Updated (Version )
Classification: ☐ Full Briefer   ☐ Short-Form Briefer

Section 1 — Brief Description of the Measure

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.1)

Purpose: Give the MP a complete snapshot of the bill — what it does, where it stands, and what action is needed next.

What to include: Bill number, title, author(s), committee referral, and current legislative stage; a 2-4 paragraph summary covering subject matter, key provisions, scope, and coverage; the date of the next proceeding and the immediate action required; whether the MP will author, co-author, or decline authorship.

Sources: Bills and Index Division records; BTA Parliament website Order of Business; the filed bill text and its Explanatory Note.
Bill/Resolution No.:
Long Title:
Short Title:
Author(s):
Co-Author(s):
Date Filed:
Committee Referred To:
Type: ☐ Government Bill   ☐ Private Member Bill   ☐ Resolution
Status: (check the current stage below)

Legislative Stage:

Stage Check Session No.
Proposal Stage (pre-filing) ☐
First Reading / Order of Business (for co-authorship) ☐ Session No.
Committee Referral ☐
Public Consultation ☐
Committee Deliberation ☐
Committee Report / Second Reading / Plenary ☐ Session No.
Third Reading / Plenary Vote ☐ Session No.
Other: ☐
Date of Next Proceeding:
Immediate Action Needed: (check one)
Action Check
Will be under “Business for the Day” / Simple Resolution ☐
Will be referred to a Committee / TWG ☐
Will undergo public consultation ☐
Will be deferred ☐
Other: ☐
Will MP co-author? ☐ Principal Author   ☐ Co-Author   ☐ Will not author

Summary of the Measure:

(In two to four paragraphs, describe what the bill does: subject matter, key provisions, scope and coverage, obligations created, institutions established, rights conferred.)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Section 2 — Related Laws, Issuances, and Specific Provisions

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.2)

Purpose: Map every existing law the bill interacts with and flag conflicts early.

What to include: Specific BOL articles/sections the bill implements, supplements, or conflicts with; existing BAAs, national laws, and IRRs that overlap; analysis of conflicts, overlaps, or gaps; Shari’ah considerations if the bill touches personal law, family relations, or Islamic finance.

Sources: BOL (RA 11054) full text; Bills and Index Division BAA records (81 enacted BAAs); Lawphil.net for national laws; BARMM Official Gazette.

Identify every law, issuance, or regulation that the bill interacts with. For each, state the specific provision and explain the relationship.

Law / Issuance Relevant Provisions Relationship to Proposed Measure
BOL (RA 11054) — Art. …, Sec. … ☐ Implements   ☐ Supplements   ☐ Conflicts

Analysis of Conflicts or Overlaps:

 

 

 

Shari’ah Considerations:

Question Assessment
Does the bill affect personal, family, or property relations involving Muslims? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
If yes, does the bill respect the Shari’ah court jurisdiction under BOL Article X? ☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ N/A
Does the bill affect Islamic banking, finance, or commercial transactions? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Are there provisions that may conflict with Islamic principles? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
If yes, describe:

Section 3 — Impact Assessment and Alignment with the MP’s Agenda

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.3)

Purpose: Assess whether the bill advances or conflicts with the MP’s legislative agenda and the Bangsamoro Development Plan.

What to include: Alignment with the MP’s stated priorities and committee assignments; social, economic, and institutional impact; alignment with the six BDP 2023-2028 goals; GAD assessment covering women, youth, children, PWDs, IPs, and IDPs.

Sources: MP’s filed legislative agenda; BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 2 (goals and strategies); PSA BARMM data; agency reports on affected sectors.

Alignment with the MP’s Legislative Agenda:

Question Assessment
Does the bill advance the MP’s stated legislative priorities?
Does the bill benefit or burden the MP’s constituency?
Does the bill align with the MP’s committee work?
What are the political dimensions? (Government priority? Certified as urgent? Allied or rival sponsor?)

Broader Impact Assessment:

Dimension Assessment
Social Impact — Who benefits? Who bears the costs?
Economic Impact — Investment, employment, costs to businesses and households?
Institutional Impact — New agencies, expanded mandates, restructuring required?

BDP 2023-2028 Alignment (check all goals the bill serves):

Goal How the Bill Serves This Goal
☐ Goal 1 — Stable, just, and accountable government
☐ Goal 2 — Equitable, competitive, and robust economy
☐ Goal 3 — Peaceful, safe, and resilient communities
☐ Goal 4 — Inclusive, responsive, and quality social services
☐ Goal 5 — Rich and diverse Bangsamoro culture and identity
☐ Goal 6 — Strategic, adequate, and climate-resilient infrastructure

Gender and Development (GAD) Assessment:

Question Assessment
Does the bill affect women and men differently? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Does the bill specifically address the needs of women, youth, children, or PWDs? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Does the bill affect indigenous peoples within the Bangsamoro? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Does the bill affect internally displaced persons (IDPs)? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
If the bill creates programs, does it require sex-disaggregated data and reporting? ☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ N/A

Section 4 — Implementing Agencies

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.4)

Purpose: Identify every agency responsible for implementation and flag capacity gaps that could prevent the bill from working.

What to include: Each agency’s name, role under the bill, and whether its current mandate covers the new function; capacity assessment for each agency; coordination requirements between agencies; specific gaps requiring transition periods or additional funding.

Sources: BARMM organizational structure; agency annual reports; BEP staffing and budget data; consultations with agency staff.

Agency Role Under the Bill Current Mandate Covers This? Capacity Assessment
☐ Yes   ☐ Partially   ☐ No ☐ Adequate   ☐ Limited   ☐ Insufficient
☐ Yes   ☐ Partially   ☐ No ☐ Adequate   ☐ Limited   ☐ Insufficient
☐ Yes   ☐ Partially   ☐ No ☐ Adequate   ☐ Limited   ☐ Insufficient
☐ Yes   ☐ Partially   ☐ No ☐ Adequate   ☐ Limited   ☐ Insufficient

Coordination Requirements:

 

 

Capacity Gaps Identified:

 

 

Section 5 — Implementation Mechanisms and Timeline

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.5)

Purpose: Evaluate how the bill transitions from enacted law to operational reality and flag timeline gaps.

What to include: Whether an IRR is required, who prepares it, and whether the deadline is realistic; phase-in provisions, reporting requirements, and sunset clauses; the gap between effectivity and agency readiness.

Sources: The bill text (effectivity and IRR provisions); comparable BAAs and their IRR timelines; agency consultations on implementation readiness.

Mechanism Details
IRR Required? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
IRR Deadline: days from effectivity
Responsible for IRR:
Phase-In Provisions? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Phase-In Description:
Reporting Requirements? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Reports To:
Reporting Frequency:
Sunset Clause? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
Expiry Date / Review Period:

Gap Between Effectivity and Implementation Readiness:

 

 

Section 6 — Budgetary and Financial Implications

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.6)

Purpose: Quantify the bill’s fiscal impact so the MP can assess affordability and sustainability.

What to include: Estimated costs by PS, MOOE, and CO; whether costs are recurring or one-time; identified funding source; revenue implications if applicable; fiscal sustainability assessment.

Sources: Current BEP for baseline figures; MFBM salary schedules; agency budget submissions; BAA 84 (Budget System Act) for fiscal framework rules.

Category Estimated Cost Recurring / One-Time Notes
Personnel Services (new positions, salary upgrades) ☐ Recurring   ☐ One-Time
MOOE (operations, training, travel) ☐ Recurring   ☐ One-Time
Capital Outlay (facilities, equipment, vehicles) ☐ Recurring   ☐ One-Time
Total Estimated Cost
Funding Source Identified in the Bill? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
If Yes, Source:
Amount Appropriated in the Bill:
Revenue Implications (if revenue measure):
Fiscal Sustainability Assessment:

 

 

Section 7 — Highlights of Previous Proceedings

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.3.7)

Purpose: Brief the MP on what has already happened so they do not repeat settled questions or miss live issues.

What to include: Key issues raised in prior committee hearings and who raised them; committee amendments already adopted; unresolved issues flagged for plenary.

Sources: Committee hearing minutes from the Parliament Secretariat; Committee Report (if issued); session journals.

Proceeding Date Key Issues Raised Outcome
Committee Hearing
Committee Hearing (2nd)
Plenary (if applicable)

Committee Amendments Made:

 

 

Unresolved Issues:

 

 

Section 8 — Interpellation Questions

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.4)

Purpose: Prepare the MP for interpellation with structured question sequences that expose weaknesses and build the case for amendments.

What to include: 3-7 interpellation sequences using the six-part structure (context, question, anticipated answer, manifestation if satisfactory, manifestation if evasive, proposed amendment with justification); questions ranked by importance — most critical first.

Sources: The bill as amended by committee; Committee Report; BOL provisions on legislative authority; BEP data for budget questions; agency consultations for implementation questions.

Prepare three to seven interpellation sequences. Each follows the six-part structure. Arrange in order of importance — most critical questions first.

Question No. 1

Part Content
Introduction / Context (“Hugot”)
Question
Anticipated Answer
Manifestation (if answer is satisfactory)
Manifestation (if answer is evasive or reveals a gap)
Proposed Amendment (if applicable)
Justification for Amendment

Question No. 2

Part Content
Introduction / Context (“Hugot”)
Question
Anticipated Answer
Manifestation (if answer is satisfactory)
Manifestation (if answer is evasive or reveals a gap)
Proposed Amendment (if applicable)
Justification for Amendment

Question No. 3

Part Content
Introduction / Context (“Hugot”)
Question
Anticipated Answer
Manifestation (if answer is satisfactory)
Manifestation (if answer is evasive or reveals a gap)
Proposed Amendment (if applicable)
Justification for Amendment

(Add additional question sequences as needed. Prepare up to seven for complex bills.)

Section 9 — Summary of Proposed Amendments

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.5)

Purpose: Give the MP a ready-to-file table of amendments prioritized by importance.

What to include: Each amendment in standard floor format (insertion, deletion, or substitution); current text, proposed new text, and a one-paragraph justification; priority ranking (High / Medium / Low) so the MP knows which to propose first if time is limited.

Sources: Issues identified in Sections 2-6 of this briefer; the committee-amended bill text; BOL and existing BAAs for legal consistency checks.

No. Section Current Text Proposed Amendment Justification Priority
1 ☐ High   ☐ Medium   ☐ Low
2 ☐ High   ☐ Medium   ☐ Low
3 ☐ High   ☐ Medium   ☐ Low
4 ☐ High   ☐ Medium   ☐ Low
5 ☐ High   ☐ Medium   ☐ Low

Section 10 — MP’s Position — Brief Speech / Manifestation

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.6.1)

Purpose: Draft a 2-4 minute floor speech stating the MP’s position, grounded in the analysis.

What to include: A recommended position (Support / Support with Amendments / Oppose / Reserve Position); a speech opening with the position, presenting 2-3 key points with specific section references, and closing with the intended vote.

Sources: Analysis from Sections 2-6; the MP’s legislative agenda for alignment framing; BDP goals for policy grounding.

Recommended Position: ☐ Support   ☐ Support with Amendments   ☐ Oppose   ☐ Reserve Position

Draft Speech (2-4 minutes):

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Section 11 — 1-Minute Vote Manifestation

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.6.2)

Purpose: Prepare a short statement the MP can deliver when they want their vote recorded without a full speech.

What to include: A 3-5 sentence draft stating the position, the reason, and the vote; a specific reference to a BOL provision, BDP goal, or fiscal fact that anchors the statement.

Sources: The recommended position from Section 10; BOL provisions relevant to the bill.

Draft (3-5 sentences):

 

 

 

 

Section 12 — Matters for Information of the MP

(Refer to Chapter 8, Section 8.7)

Purpose: Give the MP context that does not fit in the formal analysis but could affect how they engage during proceedings.

What to include: Stakeholder positions (CSOs, business groups, LGU leagues); media coverage and Chief Minister statements; pending court cases and constituency feedback; comparative legislation from Congress or other LGUs; timing or procedural considerations.

Sources: BARMM news outlets and social media; BTA Parliament website for parallel bills; Supreme Court e-Library; constituency liaison reports.

Category Details
Stakeholder Positions
Media Coverage
Related Developments (Chief Minister statements, committee chair signals, parallel bills)
Political Dynamics
Pending Court Cases
Constituency Feedback
Comparative Legislation (Congress, other regions, LGUs)
Expert Opinions
Timing / Procedural Considerations

Section 13 — Action Log

Purpose: Track every action on the briefer from receipt to post-proceeding update.

What to include: Date, action, responsible person, and status for each milestone; update status checkboxes as work progresses.

Sources: Internal office workflow; Parliament Secretariat calendar for hearing dates; the MP’s schedule for submission deadlines.

Date Action Responsible Status
Bill received / identified for analysis ☐ Complete
Legislative analysis prepared ☐ Complete   ☐ In Progress
Interpellation questions drafted ☐ Complete   ☐ In Progress
Proposed amendments drafted ☐ Complete   ☐ In Progress
Briefer submitted to MP for review ☐ Complete   ☐ Pending
Committee hearing ☐ Complete   ☐ Upcoming —
Plenary deliberation ☐ Complete   ☐ TBD
Post-proceeding update ☐ Complete   ☐ Pending

Section 14 — Supervisory Review

Purpose: Ensure the briefer passes through quality review before reaching the MP.

What to include: First and second review entries with reviewer name, notes, date, and time; Chief of Staff clearance as the final gate.

Sources: Office SOPs; the reviewer’s subject-matter expertise and the Quality Review Checklist (Appendix E).

Action Reviewer Review / Clearance Notes Date Time
First Review / Clearance
Second Review / Clearance
Chief of Staff Clearance

Section 15 — Approval

Purpose: Formally present the briefer to the MP for decision.

What to include: Preparer name, designation, date, and recommended position; space for the MP’s comments; decision options (approve, modify, or return).

Sources: The completed briefer (Sections 1-14); verbal or written feedback from any pre-briefing discussion.
Prepared by:
Designation:
Date Submitted:
Recommended Position: ☐ Support   ☐ Support with Amendments   ☐ Oppose   ☐ Reserve Position

Comments by the MP:

  1.  
  2.  
  3.  

MP’s Decision:
☐ Approved as recommended
☐ Approved with modifications:
☐ Disapproved / Return for further study
MP’s Signature:
Date:

This template is designed to be reproduced and used as a standalone document. Permission is granted to photocopy or print this appendix for use in legislative offices of the Bangsamoro Parliament.

—

Appendix G — Budget Briefer Template

This template follows the Completed Staff Work (CSW) framework described in Chapter 9. It is designed to be filled in for each agency whose budget the MP will review during the annual Bangsamoro Expenditure Program (BEP) deliberation — whether in Committee on Finance hearings or in plenary.

The template covers sixteen sections. For detailed guidance on how to complete each section, refer to the corresponding sections in Chapter 9.

BUDGET BRIEFER

Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament

Control Information
Control No.: BB--
Date Prepared:
Prepared by:
Designation:
Office of MP:
Version: ☐ Initial   ☐ Updated (Version )
For: ☐ Committee on Finance Hearing   ☐ Plenary Deliberation

Section 1 — Ministry / Agency / Office

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.3.1)

Purpose: Identify the agency precisely so the MP knows exactly which entity’s budget is under review.

What to include: Full name, abbreviation, agency type, BEP code and page number; head of agency, appointment date, and organizational scope; calculate fill rate (filled / authorized positions) and note staffing anomalies.

Sources: BEP current FY; agency organizational chart or annual report; BTA Parliament records for agency heads.
Full Name:
Abbreviation:
Type: ☐ Ministry   ☐ Office under OCM   ☐ Parliamentary Office   ☐ GOCC   ☐ Constitutional Body   ☐ Other:
Head of Agency:
Date of Appointment:
BEP Code:
BEP Page Number:
No. of Divisions/Bureaus:
No. of Field Offices:
Provinces Covered:
Total Authorized Positions:
Total Filled Positions:
Fill Rate: %

Organizational Notes:

 

 

Section 2 — Mandate and Legal Basis

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.3.2)

Purpose: Establish the agency’s legal mandate so the MP can test whether the budget matches what the law requires.

What to include: The creating law (BAA, executive order, or BOL provision); core functions in 2-3 sentences; whether the mandate changed since the last budget cycle and the fiscal impact of any change.

Sources: BAA No. 13 (Administrative Code); specific creating BAAs from the Bills and Index Division; BOL Articles IV-XII for constitutionally mandated bodies.
Primary Legal Basis:
Creating Law / Issuance:

Core Functions (2-3 sentences):

 

 

 
Has the mandate changed since the last budget cycle? ☐ Yes   ☐ No
If yes, describe the change and its fiscal impact:

 

 

Section 3 — Overall Development Goals

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.3.3)

Purpose: Map the agency to BDP 2023-2028 goals so the MP can assess strategic alignment.

What to include: Check which of the six BDP goals the agency serves (primary and secondary); write an alignment assessment comparing claimed goals against actual budget distribution (see Section 5).

Sources: BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 2 (goals and strategies); agency BEP narrative; BEDC sectoral committee reports.

Check all BDP 2023-2028 goals the agency primarily serves:

Goal Primary?
☐ Goal 1 — Stable, just, and accountable government ☐ Primary   ☐ Secondary
☐ Goal 2 — Equitable, competitive, and robust economy ☐ Primary   ☐ Secondary
☐ Goal 3 — Peaceful, safe, and resilient Bangsamoro communities ☐ Primary   ☐ Secondary
☐ Goal 4 — Inclusive, responsive, and quality social services ☐ Primary   ☐ Secondary
☐ Goal 5 — Rich and diverse Bangsamoro culture and identity preserved and recognized ☐ Primary   ☐ Secondary
☐ Goal 6 — Strategic, adequate, and climate-resilient infrastructure ☐ Primary   ☐ Secondary

Alignment Assessment:

(Compare what the agency claims to prioritize (goals checked above) against where its money actually goes (Section 5). If an agency checks “Goal 4 — Social Services” but allocates 80% to administrative MOOE, that is an alignment gap. Note any such gaps below.)

 

 

Section 4 — Organizational Outcomes and Key Performance Indicators

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.4)

Purpose: Establish the agency’s performance track record before examining budget numbers.

What to include: KPIs with targets, baselines, and whether each was met; root cause analyses for missed KPIs; check whether the agency tracks SQI and CSI — absence is itself a finding worth raising.

Sources: Agency annual reports; BEP performance narratives; BDP results matrix (BEDC/BPDA); COA annual audit reports.

KPI Target Baseline Met? BDP Goal
☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ Partial
☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ Partial
☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ Partial
☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ Partial
☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ Partial
☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ Partial

For KPIs that fell short of target — root cause analysis:

(For each KPI marked “No” or “Partial” above, write 2-3 sentences: what was the target, what was actually achieved, and why the gap exists. Sources: agency annual reports, BEP narratives, COA findings.)

 

 

 

Service Quality and Satisfaction:

(Check whether the agency tracks these. If the agency does not have an SQI or CSI, mark “No” and note “N/A — agency does not track this metric.” This is itself a finding worth raising during the hearing.)

Dimension Agency Tracks? Assessment
Service Quality Index (SQI) ☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ N/A
— Accessibility (ease of reaching/using services)
— Reliability (consistent, accurate delivery)
— Timeliness (speed, punctuality of delivery)
— Responsiveness (willingness to assist promptly)
— Assurance (trust, competence, security)
— Sustainability (environmental and social responsibility)
Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) ☐ Yes   ☐ No   ☐ N/A
— Survey or feedback system in place?
— Most recent score or result:

Section 5 — Budget Comparison

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.5)

Purpose: Provide the quantitative core of the briefer — comparing this year’s request to previous allocations and actual spending.

What to include: Three-year budget comparison (PS, MOOE, CO, GAD) with variances; absorption rates for previous FY (flag rates below 80%); PS, MOOE, and CO detail breakdowns; line-item variance analysis for any item changing more than 20%; verify GAD meets 5% minimum under BAA 84.

Sources: BEP current and previous FY; General Appropriations Acts; MFBM budget execution reports; COA audit observations.

5a. Three-Year Budget Comparison

Category FY (Year-2) FY (Year-1) FY (Current) Variance
PS
MOOE
CO
Total
GAD

3-Year Trend: ☐ Increasing   ☐ Stable   ☐ Decreasing GAD meets 5% minimum? ☐ Yes   ☐ No

5b. Absorption Capacity (Previous FY)

Category Allocation Actual Expenditure Absorption Rate
PS
MOOE
CO
Total

5c. Personnel Services Detail

Item Details
New positions requested positions
Position titles and salary grades
Administrative-to-program delivery ratio :
Positions vacant > 12 months
Overtime/allowance as % of base salaries %

5d. MOOE Detail

Item Details
Program delivery MOOE vs. administrative MOOE % : %
Travel budget as % of total MOOE %
Training/capacity-building budget
Consultant fees

5e. Capital Outlay Detail

Project Prev FY Alloc Status Current FY Total Cost

CO Utilization Rate (3-Year): FY : %   FY : %   FY : %

5f. Line-Item Variance Analysis

Line Item FY (Year-2) Actual FY (Year-1) Actual FY (Current) Proposed Variance (Year-1 to Current) Favorable? Explanation
☐ F   ☐ U
☐ F   ☐ U
☐ F   ☐ U
☐ F   ☐ U
☐ F   ☐ U

(For any line item that changed by more than 20% from the previous year, explain the variance in the table above. F = Favorable, U = Unfavorable.)

Mid-Year Adjustments or Reallocations (Previous FY):

 

 

Internal Controls Assessment:

 

 

Section 6 — Existing PPAS (Tier 1)

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.6)

Purpose: Evaluate the agency’s continuing programs to determine whether current spending is producing results.

What to include: Each PPAS with type, allocation, actual spend, request, and absorption rate; budget distribution across core/support/admin/cross-cutting; program-level performance evaluation against targets.

Sources: BEP program-level breakdowns (PREXC: GAS, STO, Operations); MFBM budget execution data; agency accomplishment reports.

PPAS Type FY Alloc FY Actual FY Request Variance Absorption
☐ Core   ☐ Support   ☐ Admin   ☐ Cross-cutting
☐ Core   ☐ Support   ☐ Admin   ☐ Cross-cutting
☐ Core   ☐ Support   ☐ Admin   ☐ Cross-cutting
☐ Core   ☐ Support   ☐ Admin   ☐ Cross-cutting
☐ Core   ☐ Support   ☐ Admin   ☐ Cross-cutting

Budget Distribution: Core Mandate %   Support %   Administrative %   Cross-cutting %

Performance Evaluation of Key Tier 1 Programs:

Program Target Actual Met? Issues
☐ Yes   ☐ Partial   ☐ No
☐ Yes   ☐ Partial   ☐ No
☐ Yes   ☐ Partial   ☐ No

Outcome indicators for key programs (1-2 sentences per program: what indicator was tracked, what was achieved, and whether the budget was the limiting factor):

 

 

Section 7 — New and Expanded PPAS (Tier 2)

(Refer to Chapter 9, Section 9.7)

Purpose: Scrutinize new and expanded proposals that lack a performance track record.

What to include: For each new program: type, priority, PS/MOOE/CO breakdown, rationale, and expected outcomes; complete the scrutiny table (evidence of need, legal basis, BEDC endorsement, feasibility study, targets, exit strategy, lifecycle cost, consequence if not funded).

Sources: Agency Tier 2 budget submission forms (per BBM guidelines); BEDC endorsement letters; BPDA certification; Budget Endorsing Authority clearances.

New/Expanded Program 1:

Field Details
PPAS Name:
Type: ☐ New Initiative   ☐ Expansion   ☐ Pilot   ☐ One-Time Project
Priority: ☐ High   ☐ Medium   ☐ Low
Budget: PS:   MOOE:   CO:
Rationale:
